1335	\begin{lemma}
1336	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1337	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1338	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1339	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1340	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1341	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1342	  instance
1343	  \[
1344	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1345	  \]
1346	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1347	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1348	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1349	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1350	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1351	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1352	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1353	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1354	  category
1355	  \[
1356	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1357	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1358	  \]
1359	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1360	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1361	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1362	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1363	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1364	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1365	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1366	  composites.
1367	
1368	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it depends on the sole open obligation
1369	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1370	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1371	\end{lemma}
1372	
1373	\begin{proof}
1374	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1375	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1376	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1377	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1378	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1379	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1380	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1381	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1382	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1383	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1384	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1385	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1386	  at any point.
1387	\end{proof}

1389	\begin{lemma}
1390	
1391	\leanok
1392	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1393	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1396	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1397	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1398	  \[
1399	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1400	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1401	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1402	  \]
1403	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1404	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1405	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1406	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1407	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1408	  ever required.
1409	
1410	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1411	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1412	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1413	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1414	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1415	\end{lemma}
1416	
1417	\begin{proof}
1418	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1419	  \textbf{Status (route mismatch, deferred).} The \emph{current} realization of this
1420	  associator uses the route-(d) whiskering composite
1421	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} and the sheafification
1422	  bridge \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}); through
1423	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- whose left-whiskering component is
1424	  itself unproved --- it transitively depends on an open proof obligation and is
1425	  therefore \emph{not} axiom-clean. The gluing route described below is the
1426	  \emph{intended} obligation-free realization, but discharging it requires
1427	  \emph{morphism-level descent for \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)}: gluing a family of
1428	  local isomorphisms, agreeing on overlaps, into a single global isomorphism of
1429	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. That is the same descent-infrastructure family
1430	  the \(\otimes\)-inverse needs (\cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}); accordingly, the
1431	  associator re-route \emph{and} the deletion of the vestigial whiskering/stalk
1432	  apparatus are deferred jointly with the dual block, and are not available with the
1433	  current infrastructure. The gluing description below records the target realization.
1434	
1435	  The isomorphism is built by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through the
1436	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, with no
1437	  recourse to any whiskering or stalk apparatus. Fix a
1438	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1439	  composite
1440	  \[
1441	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1442	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1443	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1444	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1445	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1446	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1447	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1448	  \]
1449	  in which:
1450	  \begin{itemize}
1451	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1452	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1453	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1454	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1455	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1456	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1457	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1458	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1459	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1460	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1461	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1462	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1463	  \end{itemize}
1464	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1465	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1466	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1467	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1468	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1469	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1470	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1471	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1472	  composites agree on the overlap.
1473	
1474	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1475	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1476	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1477	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1478	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1479	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1480	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1481	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1482	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1483	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1484	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1485	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1486	
1487	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1488	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1489	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1490	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1491	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1492	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1493	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1494	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1495	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1496	  specialisation can replace it. The vestigial
1497	  whiskering / \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus
1498	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}) plays
1499	  no part in this proof and is superseded. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1500	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1501	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1502	\end{proof}

1556	\begin{lemma}
1557	
1558	\leanok
1559	[Inverse of an invertible module]
1560	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
1561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
1562	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
1563	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1564	  % NOTE: ON the critical path under the PRIMARY locally-trivial group law (it
1565	  % supplies inverses to lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, in whose uses it sits).
1566	  % Only under the route-(e) fallback is it dispensable: there the group law uses
1567	  % the tensor-invertibility predicate IsInvertible (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible),
1568	  % which carries the inverse existentially, so no separate ``a tensor inverse

1570	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible

1576	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1577	  % "\begin{lemma}
1578	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
1579	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1580	  % ...
1581	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is

1595	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1596	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction
1597	  is an isomorphism) and Definition~\texttt{definition-pic} (the Picard group is
1598	  the abelian group of iso-classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules under

1600	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1601	  bundle. The dual
1602	  \[
1603	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1604	  \]
1605	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1606	  isomorphism

1610	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1611	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).

1614	\begin{proof}
1615	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1616	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
1617	  \textbf{Infrastructure-blocked.} This statement is \emph{not} realizable with the
1618	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module infrastructure presently available: its construction
1619	  depends on a \emph{sheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules}
1620	  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\), an object that does not yet
1621	  exist at the presheaf, sheaf, or general-categorical level (there is no
1622	  monoidal-closed / internal-hom / dual structure on
1623	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) or \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), and no object-level
1624	  descent assembling a global \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module from local pieces). The dual
1625	  object \(L^{-1}\) therefore cannot even be \emph{named} until that infrastructure
1626	  is built. The construction of the required internal hom, its dual, and its
1627	  evaluation is decomposed into individually-formalizable sub-steps in
1628	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} through
1629	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}); the present lemma is realized once those land,
1630	  and its Lean body is a placeholder until then.
1631	
1632	  We record the intended mathematical route, all three steps of which are correct
1633	  modulo the missing object. \emph{The route is as follows.} Take
1634	  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) to be the
1635	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, and let
1636	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) be the evaluation of
1637	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}.
1638	
1639	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1640	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1641	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1642	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1643	  \[
1644	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1645	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1646	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1647	  \]
1648	  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
1649	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1650	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1651	
1652	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1653	  morphism
1654	  \[
1655	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1656	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1657	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1658	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1659	  \]
1660	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1661	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1662	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1663	  \[
1664	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1665	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1666	  \]
1667	  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
1668	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1669	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1670	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1671	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1672	
1673	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1674	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1675	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1676	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1677	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1678	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1679	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1680	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1681	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1682	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).
1683	
1684	  Consequently, \emph{once \(L^{-1}\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) are available from
1685	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}}, every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor
1686	  inverse \(L^{-1}\) under \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of
1687	  line bundles is an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1688	  The local-triviality of \(L^{-1}\) (Step~1) is \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} and
1689	  the local-iso\(\Rightarrow\)global-iso passage (Step~3) is the already-closed
1690	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the single non-available input is the dual object
1691	  and its evaluation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
1692	\end{proof}
1693	
1694	\begin{lemma}
1695	
1696	\leanok
1697	[Closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial carrier
1698	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
1699	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
1701	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1702	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1703	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. The carrier is the
1704	  \emph{locally-trivial} subtype
1705	  \[
1706	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1707	    \;\;=\;\;
1708	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
1709	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}
1710	  \]
1711	  introduced in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. The bifunctor
1712	  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
1713	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
1714	  if \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M'\) then
1715	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\). Hence \(\otimes\)
1716	  closes on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\), supplying the binary
1717	  operation the relative group law multiplies with.
1718	\end{lemma}
1719	
1720	\begin{proof}
1721	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1722	  Closure is exactly
1723	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}: the tensor product of two
1724	  locally-trivial rank-one modules is again locally trivial of rank one
1725	  (\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_isLocallyTrivial}\)). The Lean construction
1726	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjOnProduct}\) applies this directly to a pair of objects of
1727	  the \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) subtype \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\),
1728	  returning the tensor with its locally-trivial witness; no
1729	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate and no coherence isomorphism enters this
1730	  closure statement. (The \(\otimes\)-invertibility carrier
1731	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} is a
1732	  separate predicate, connected to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) only by an
1733	  off-critical-path equivalence; the units-group packaging is
1734	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, not this lemma.)
1735	\end{proof}
1736	
1737	\begin{lemma}
1738	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1739	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1740	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1741	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1742	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1743	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1744	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1745	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1746	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1747	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1748	  functor
1749	  \[
1750	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1751	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1752	  \]
1753	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1754	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1755	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1756	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1757	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1758	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1759	  canonical isomorphism
1760	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1761	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1762	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1763	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1764	\end{lemma}
1765	
1766	\begin{proof}
1767	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1768	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1769	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1770	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1771	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1772	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1773	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1774	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1775	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1776	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1777	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1778	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1779	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1780	  same cover.
1781	\end{proof}

1783	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1784	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1785	
1786	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1787	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1788	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1789	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1790	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1791	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1792	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
1793	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
1794	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
1795	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
1796	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
1797	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
1798	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1799	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1803	  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
1804	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
1805	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1806	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1807	  % modules), Definition 01CS (definition-invertible) + Lemma 0B8K
1808	  % (lemma-invertible, the "$\exists\,\mathcal{N}$" characterisation)
1809	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4079).
1810	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1811	  % "\begin{definition}
1812	  % \label{definition-invertible}
1813	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
1814	  % {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
1815	  % of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
1816	  % the functor
1817	  % $$
1818	  % \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
1819	  % \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
1820	  % $$
1821	  % is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
1822	  % {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1823	  % to $\mathcal{O}_X$.
1824	  % \end{definition}
1825	  % [\dots]
1826	  % \begin{lemma}
1827	  % \label{lemma-invertible}
1828	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $\mathcal{L}$
1829	  % be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Equivalent are
1830	  % \begin{enumerate}
1831	  % \item $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible, and

1833	  % such that

1835	  % \end{enumerate}
1836	  % In this case $\mathcal{L}$ is locally a direct summand of a finite free

1840	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1841	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CS and Lemma~0B8K. An invertible
1842	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is one for which \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1843	  (-)\) is an equivalence; equivalently there exists \(\mathcal{N}\) with
1844	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and
1845	  then \(\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
1846	  \mathcal{O}_X)\).}
1847	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
1848	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1849	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
1850	  \[
1851	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
1852	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1853	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
1854	  \]
1855	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
1856	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
1857	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
1858	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
1859	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
1860	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
1861	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
1862	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
1863	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
1864	  \(\otimes_X\).
1865	\end{definition}

1916	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1917	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1918	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1919	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1920	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1921	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1922	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1923	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1924	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1925	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1926	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1927	  01CR).
1928	
1929	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1930	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1931	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1932	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1933	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1934	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1935	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1936	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1937	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1938	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1939	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1940	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1941	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1942	
1943	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1944	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1945	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1946	  \[
1947	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1948	    \quad
1949	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1950	    \quad
1951	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1952	    \quad
1953	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1954	  \]
1955	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1956	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1957	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1958	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1959	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1960	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1961	
1962	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1963	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1964	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1965	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1966	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1967	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1968	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1969	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1970	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1971	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1972	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1973	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1974	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1975	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1976	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1977	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1978	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1979	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1980	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1981	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1982	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1983	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1984	  pinned commit.
1985	
1986	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1987	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1988	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1989	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1990	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1991	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1992	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1993	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1994	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1995	
1996	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1997	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1998	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1999	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
2000	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
2001	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
2002	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
2003	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
2004	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
2005	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160

162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)

208	[Naturality respects the group structure]
209	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
213	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
214	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
215	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
216	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
217	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
220	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of

225	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by

227	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
228	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
229	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
230	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
231	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of

236	  homomorphisms.

238	
239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
240	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No

259	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
260	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
261	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
262	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
263	  structure sheaf, hence the map

327	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
328	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
329	
330	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
331	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
332	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
333	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
334	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
335	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
336	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
337	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
338	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.

374	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
375	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
376	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
377	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
378	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
379	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
380	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):
381	  \[
382	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;
383	       \bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\bigr)^{\sim_{\et}}.
384	  \]
385	  Equivalently, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is the unique (up to canonical
386	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)
387	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
388	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
389	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
390	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
391	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
392	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
393	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
394	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
395	\end{definition}

499	
500	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
501	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical
502	  sheafification unit) supplies, in particular, a morphism of
503	  presheaves \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); the

512	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
513	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
514	
515	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
516	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
517	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
518	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
519	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
520	  % property)
521	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
522	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
523	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
524	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
525	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
526	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
527	  % topologies  by
528	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
529	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
530	  %  \end{dfn}
531	  %  [\dots]
532	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
533	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
534	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
535	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
536	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
537	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
538	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
539	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
540	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
541	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
542	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
543	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
544	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
545	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
546	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
547	  \[
548	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
549	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
550	  \]
551	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
552	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
553	  of presheaves of abelian groups
554	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
555	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
556	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
557	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
558	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
559	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
560	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
561	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
562	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
563	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
564	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
565	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
566	  \(\eta\).
567	\end{theorem}
568	
569	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
570	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
571	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
572	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
573	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
574	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
575	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
576	\begin{proof}
577	
578	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
579	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
580	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
581	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
582	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
583	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
584	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
585	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
586	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
587	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
588	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
589	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
590	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
591	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
592	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
593	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
594	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
595	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
596	\end{proof}

701	\paragraph{Gate annotation (iter-198 refresh).}
702	The residual prover gate inside
703	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} (the file
704	implementing the six declarations above) is \emph{not} on the
705	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier --- that typed sorry was closed
706	in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean} during
707	the iter-188 unpacking, where
708	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} was concretised as the subtype
709	\(\{N : (\texttt{pullback}\ \pi_C\ \pi_T).\mathtt{Modules}\ \mathbin{|}\
710	\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\ N\}\) and
711	\texttt{pullbackAlongProjection} / \texttt{pullback\_pullback\_eq} were
712	filled axiom-cleanly using \texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullback},
713	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}, and
714	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}.
715	
716	The genuine residual gate is the abelian-group instance on the
717	quotient \texttt{Quotient (preimage\_subgroup πC πT)} (the file-local
718	sorry scaffold near \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L205--L235, which the
719	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup} declaration is
720	pinned to). That instance in turn depends on a tensor-product
721	\texttt{AddCommGroup} structure on \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}:
722	forming the abelian-group law on isomorphism classes
723	\([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes L']\) requires a tensor-product operation
724	on the carrier and an inverse for invertibles. Mathlib at the pinned
725	commit \texttt{b80f227} ships
726	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} (a tensor product at
727	the level of \texttt{PresheafOfModules}), but does \emph{not} expose a
728	monoidal-category structure on the higher-level
729	\texttt{Scheme.Modules} category; this is the gap explicitly flagged
730	in \texttt{LineBundlePullback.lean}, L344--L346, with the comment
731	``Mathlib ships \texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} but no
732	monoidal structure on \texttt{Scheme.Modules} directly at
733	\texttt{b80f227}'' --- the \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure
734	gap.
735	
736	Modulo that single file-local sorry, the five other prover-gate
737	sorries at \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L287--L482 (the bodies of
738	\texttt{PicSharp},
739	\texttt{PicSharp.functorial},
740	\texttt{PicSharp.presheaf},
741	\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf}, and
742	\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure}) close axiom-cleanly: each
743	consumes \texttt{addCommGroup} as a typeclass hypothesis and threads
744	the resulting group structure through the standard Mathlib pattern
745	(\texttt{QuotientAddGroup} for the quotient, \texttt{Functor.mk} for
746	the presheaf packaging, \texttt{Quotient.lift} respecting the
747	\texttt{AddCommGroup} structure for the functorial action, and
748	\texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat}
749	for the \'etale sheafification with its abelian-group target).
750	Once the \texttt{addCommGroup} instance is provided, the file
751	collapses from six sorries to one, and the one remaining sorry is
752	gated only on the Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure
753	gap rather than on any project-side mathematics (making it an
754	upstream Mathlib-build-mode task, not an A.1.c mathematical gap).
755	
756	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by
757	the slogan: the FGA Picard scheme of A.2.c represents
758	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (Kleiman \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
759	\(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)''), \emph{not} \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) directly. The \'etale
760	sheafification step of this chapter is precisely what makes the
761	representability theorem in A.2.c well-posed.

763	\section{Out of scope}
764	\label{sec:relpic_out_of_scope}
765	
766	The following constructions are downstream of this chapter and live in
767	dedicated sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
768	\begin{itemize}
769	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
770	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- the
771	    Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman existence theorem is the next step but is
772	    not part of this chapter.
773	  \item \textbf{Comparison theorem $\Pic_{X/S} \hookrightarrow
774	    \Pic_{(X/S)\zar} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\et} \hookrightarrow
775	    \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$} (Kleiman \S 2, th:cmp) --- the chain of inclusions
776	    under \(\mathcal{O}_S \cong f_*\mathcal{O}_X\); we use only the
777	    \'etale-sheafified functor here, not the full comparison chain.
778	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3) --- the open subgroup
779	    scheme cut out by Hilbert polynomials, treated in a downstream chapter.
780	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4) --- consumes the full
781	    Route~A stack.
782	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on the representing object} --- once
783	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is representable (A.2.c), its representing scheme
784	    inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from the abelian-group-valued
785	    target via the Yoneda embedding; that refinement is part of A.2.c, not
786	    of this chapter.
787	\end{itemize}

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	
360	\leanok
361	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
362	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
363	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
364	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
365	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
366	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
367	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
368	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
369	  closed embedding.
370	
371	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
372	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
373	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
374	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
375	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
376	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
377	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
378	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
379	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
380	
381	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
382	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
383	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
384	
385	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
386	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
387	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
388	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
389	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
390	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
391	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
392	\end{proof}
