394	\section{Group-scheme structure}
395	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
396	
397	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
398	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
399	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
400	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
401	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
402	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
403	
404	\begin{theorem}\leanok
405	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
406	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
408	  
409	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
410	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
411	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
412	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
413	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
416	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
417	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
418	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
419	  %  topologies  by
420	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
421	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
422	  %   \end{dfn}"
423	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
424	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
425	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
426	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
427	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
428	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
429	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
430	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
431	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
432	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
433	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
434	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
435	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
436	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
437	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
438	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
439	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
440	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
441	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
442	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
443	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
444	  scheme locally of finite type.
445	\end{theorem}
446	
447	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
448	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
449	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
450	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
451	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
452	% records the standard transfer.
453	\begin{proof}
454	  
455	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
468	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
473	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}
480	
481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}\leanok
488	[The Picard scheme]
489	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
491	  
492	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
493	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
494	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
495	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
496	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
497	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
498	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
499	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
500	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
501	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
502	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
503	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing
504	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), supplied by
505	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
506	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
507	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open
508	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes.
509	\end{definition}
510	
511	\section{Lean encoding}
512	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
513	
514	The Lean target file is the new
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
516	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
517	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
518	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
519	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
520	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
521	
522	\begin{itemize}
523	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
524	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
525	    representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); it is the witness extracted
526	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
527	    \texttt{representable} below.
528	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
529	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
530	    \(k\)-schemes \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) sending a
531	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
532	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
533	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
534	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
535	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
536	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
537	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
538	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
539	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
540	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
541	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
542	    \times_k Z\( on a quasi-projective \)k\(-scheme \)Z$ to a
543	    quasi-projective quotient \(Q\), packaged in the form that consumes
544	    a \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for \(Z\) and produces
545	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
546	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
547	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
548	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
549	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
550	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
551	    \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for
552	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) via the four steps of the proof above
553	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, \(m\)-regularity bound, Abel-map
554	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
555	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
556	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
557	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
558	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
559	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
560	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
561	\end{itemize}

729	\subsection{Sorry 3 --- \texttt{instHasAbelMap} (L294)}
730	\label{subsec:sorry_has_abel_map}
731	
732	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
733	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
734	line~294; instance
735	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasAbelMap}.
736	
737	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts existence of
738	the Abel map
739	$A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ as a natural
740	transformation of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$, sending a relative
741	effective Cartier divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$ to its associated
742	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) = \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$.
743	This is the substantive content of
744	\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
745	
746	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
747	\begin{itemize}
748	  \item Natural transformation \texttt{${\rightsquigarrow}$} of
749	    functors, \texttt{Nonempty}: \emph{exists}.
750	  \item Dual of an ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$ as an invertible
751	    sheaf for a relative effective Cartier divisor: \emph{partial}.
752	    Mathlib has dual modules and dual sheaves but the specific link
753	    "ideal of a relative effective Cartier divisor is invertible and
754	    its dual is the associated line bundle" is a project-internal
755	    bridge.
756	  \item Functoriality of the assignment under pullback (Kleiman \S3
757	    Lem.~\texttt{lm:ctn}): \emph{needs-bridge}. Pullback of relative
758	    effective divisors along base-change is standard for flat-over-
759	    base divisors; Mathlib's coverage of this for the scheme-theoretic
760	    relative setting is incomplete.
761	\end{itemize}
762	
763	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the Abel map is a
764	construction at the level of line bundles and divisors, not a
765	consequence of Riemann--Roch. (Riemann--Roch tells us that the Abel
766	map for a fixed degree-$g$ pole-set is surjective onto $\Pic^g_{C/k}$;
767	this is a downstream consequence, not a prerequisite for defining the
768	map.)
769	
770	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} The component-wise definition
771	of $A_{C/k}(T)$ is: given $D \in \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)$, set
772	$A_{C/k}(T)(D) := [\mathcal{I}_D^{-1}]$ in
773	$\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$. Naturality follows from the pullback
774	formula for the dual ideal sheaf. The Lean encoding is a sequence of
775	three constructions: (a) extract the ideal sheaf of a relative
776	effective Cartier divisor, (b) dualise to get the line bundle,
777	(c) pass to the \'etale sheafified class. Each step is independent of
778	Riemann--Roch and depends only on Sorries~1 and~2 having committed to
779	a semantic witness for \texttt{picSharp} and \texttt{divFunctor}.
780	
781	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 2} (depends on Sorries~1
782	and~2; no Riemann--Roch substrate).

784	\subsection{Sorry 4 --- \texttt{instHasSmoothProperQuotient} (L349)}
785	\label{subsec:sorry_smooth_proper_quotient}
786	
787	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
788	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
789	line~349; instance constructor
790	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasSmoothProperQuotient}
791	of the iter-199 \texttt{Prop}-valued typeclass
792	\texttt{HasSmoothProperQuotient} (L338-341). The associated theorem
793	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
794	(L377) extracts \texttt{HasSmoothProperQuotient.is\_representable}
795	and is itself axiom-clean.
796	
797	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The smooth-proper quotient lemma
798	of \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}: given a surjection
799	$\alpha : Z \to P$ of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ with $Z$
800	quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$ representable, and the first
801	projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper, the quotient $P$ is
802	representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme. The proof packages
803	the Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem for
804	flat-and-proper relations on quasi-projective schemes.
805	
806	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
807	\begin{itemize}
808	  \item \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable},
809	    \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Limits.pullback}:
810	    \emph{exists}.
811	  \item \texttt{Smooth}, \texttt{IsProper} typeclasses on scheme
812	    morphisms: \emph{exists}.
813	  \item EGA IV~8.11.5 "proper monomorphism is a closed immersion":
814	    \emph{missing}. Mathlib has closed immersions, monomorphisms of
815	    schemes, and properness, but the EGA combination lemma is not yet
816	    isolated as a named result.
817	  \item Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem
818	    (flat-and-proper equivalence relation on a quasi-projective
819	    scheme is effective, with quasi-projective quotient):
820	    \emph{missing}. This is the structural heart of the lemma and is
821	    not in Mathlib in any form.
822	  \item Coequaliser uniqueness in the category of \'etale sheaves:
823	    \emph{exists} (general categorical fact, uniqueness up to unique
824	    isomorphism of coequalisers in any category).
825	\end{itemize}
826	
827	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the lemma is a
828	structural fact about quasi-projective schemes and \'etale sheaves,
829	independent of curves and Riemann--Roch. It does, however, sit on the
830	Mathlib gap "effective equivalence relations of schemes", which is a
831	substantial development in its own right (corresponds to roughly
832	Altman--Kleiman, "Compactifying the Picard scheme", and the
833	flat-and-proper descent of equivalence relations from EGA IV~17).
834	
835	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Two routes are available:
836	\begin{enumerate}
837	  \item[(a)] Wait for the Mathlib gap "effective equivalence
838	    relations of quasi-projective schemes" to be filled and import
839	    the result. This is the cleanest long-term path but is outside
840	    the project's control timeline.
841	  \item[(b)] Formalise the Altman--Kleiman construction directly in
842	    the project as a sibling Lean file, gated on the EGA IV~8.11.5
843	    lemma (proper monomorphism is a closed immersion) which can be
844	    extracted from existing Mathlib infrastructure in a focused
845	    sub-task.
846	\end{enumerate}
847	Route~(b) is the recommended iter-199+ attempt: the EGA IV~8.11.5
848	extraction is bounded and the remaining structural construction
849	(graph of the equivalence relation inside the Hilbert scheme of $Z$,
850	descent to a closed subscheme of $\Hilb_{Z/k}$) is intrinsic.
851	
852	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 2} (substantial Mathlib
853	gap; no Riemann--Roch substrate; recommended first attempt under the
854	carrier-soundness probe).

856	\subsection{Sorry 5 --- \texttt{instHasPicScheme} (L236)}
857	\label{subsec:sorry_has_pic_scheme}
858	
859	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
860	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
861	line~236; instance
862	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasPicScheme}.
863	
864	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts that there
865	exists an object $X \in \mathrm{Over}(\Spec k)$ together with a
866	\texttt{RepresentableBy} witness for $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. In
867	other words: the Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ exists. The semantic
868	content is the conclusion of Kleiman \S4 Thm.~\texttt{th:main}
869	specialised by Cor.~\texttt{cor:algsch} to $S = \Spec k$ and $X = C$
870	a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
871	
872	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
873	\begin{itemize}
874	  \item \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Nonempty},
875	    \texttt{Over}: \emph{exists}.
876	  \item \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1},
877	    \texttt{IsProper}, \texttt{GeometricallyIntegral}: \emph{exists}
878	    as typeclasses, but the rich theory needed to actually produce a
879	    representing scheme is largely missing.
880	  \item The FGA representability machinery itself (the four-step
881	    construction of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, namely
882	    Hilbert polynomial stratification, $m$-regularity bound, Abel
883	    map factorisation, smooth-proper quotient): \emph{missing}.
884	  \item Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity / $m$-regularity for
885	    cohomologically twist-vanishing sheaves: \emph{missing}.
886	  \item Riemann--Roch for line bundles on a smooth proper curve over
887	    a field (used in the $m$-regularity step to bound twists by the
888	    degree): \emph{missing} (Route~C).
889	\end{itemize}
890	
891	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} \textbf{Required}. The
892	$m$-regularity bound (Kleiman \S4 Step~2 of the proof of
893	Thm.~\texttt{th:main}) is the point at which the structural argument
894	meets Riemann--Roch: for a curve $C/k$ of genus $g$ and a line
895	bundle $\mathcal{L}$ of degree $d$, $H^1(C, \mathcal{L}(n)) = 0$ for
896	$n \geq m$ depends on $m \geq 2g - 1 - d + \deg \mathcal{O}_C(1)$, which
897	is the Serre vanishing consequence of Riemann--Roch. Specifically the
898	required result is the curve specialisation of
899	Hartshorne~III.5.2 (Serre vanishing) combined with IV.1.3
900	(Riemann--Roch for curves). Without Riemann--Roch, the $m$-regularity
901	step cannot be discharged and the FGA construction does not
902	terminate.
903	
904	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Conditional on the Route~C
905	re-engagement that the project STRATEGY.md flags as
906	substrate-blocked: once the Riemann--Roch arc lands, the $m$-regularity
907	step can be discharged via Hartshorne III.5.2 / IV.1.3, then the
908	remaining three steps (stratification, Abel-map factorisation,
909	smooth-proper quotient via Sorry~4) assemble into the existence
910	witness. Until then this sorry is the load-bearing blocker on the
911	Pic scheme arm of Route~A.
912	
913	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 3} (requires the Route~C
914	substrate; load-bearing on the entire A.2.c representability claim).

1090	\section{Out of scope}
1091	\label{sec:fga_pic_out_of_scope}
1092	
1093	This chapter packages \emph{only} the representable Picard scheme
1094	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) as an assembly of sibling-chapter inputs, plus the
1095	group-scheme refinement. The following downstream constructions live in
1096	separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
1097	\begin{itemize}
1098	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3) --- the open and
1099	    closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the unit and
1100	    consisting of those classes lying in the connected component of
1101	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\). Its dimension, smoothness, and abelian-variety
1102	    structure are the content of the A.3 chapter
1103	    \texttt{Picard\_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex} (planned), gated on this
1104	    chapter.
1105	  \item \textbf{Quot scheme representability} --- consumed by
1106	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} via \texttt{thm:quot\_representable}
1107	    (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}), not re-proved here.
1108	  \item \textbf{\'Etale sheafification of \(\Pic^\sharp\)} ---
1109	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c) produces
1110	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) from the set-valued presheaf of
1111	    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}; this chapter consumes the
1112	    sheaf as a black box.
1113	  \item \textbf{Universal Poincar\'e sheaf} (Kleiman~\S 4 Ex.~ex:univshf)
1114	    --- exists when \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (as opposed to
1115	    its \'etale sheafification) and \(C/k\) has a section; out of scope
1116	    here. The Albanese / Abel--Jacobi consumption in
1117	    \cref{chap:AbelJacobi} works with the \'etale-sheafified
1118	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) representation and does not require the
1119	    Poincar\'e sheaf.
1120	  \item \textbf{Mumford's example} (Kleiman~\S 4 eg:Mumford), counter-examples
1121	    to representability for non-integral geometric fibers --- not
1122	    relevant to a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
1123	\end{itemize}

2116	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2117	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2118	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2119	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2120	  \[
2121	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2122	  \]
2123	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2124	  abelian-group structure with addition
2125	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2126	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2127	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2128	  inverse
2129	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),

2131	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2132	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2133	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2134	  map
2135	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a

2140	\begin{proof}
2141	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2142	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2143	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2144	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2145	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2146	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2147	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2148	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2149	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2150	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2151	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2152	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2153	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2154	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2155	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2156	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2157	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2158	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2159	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2160	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2161	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2162	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2163	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2164	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2165	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2166	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2167	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2168	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2169	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2170	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2171	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2172	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2173	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2174	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2175	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2176	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2177	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2178	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2179	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2180	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2181	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2182	
2183	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2184	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2185	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2186	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2187	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2188	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2189	
2190	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2191	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2192	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2193	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2194	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2195	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2196	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2197	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2198	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2199	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2200	\end{proof}

2344	\section{Sheaf internal-hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (the \(\otimes\)-inverse
2345	  infrastructure)}
2346	\label{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}
2347	
2348	The PRIMARY group law (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) needs a
2349	\(\otimes\)-inverse for every line bundle, supplied by
2350	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} as the dual
2351	\(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The standard route
2352	constructs that dual as a special case of the \emph{sheaf internal hom} of
2353	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; over a fixed ring the internal hom of \(R\)-modules is the
2354	linear-coyoneda module \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\), with dual
2355	\(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,\mathbf{1} = M^\vee\), but for the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
2356	no such
2357	internal-hom / monoidal-closed structure exists at the
2358	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\), \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), or general-categorical
2359	level. This section decomposes the construction of the missing object into
2360	individually-formalizable sub-steps, following the slice presentation of the internal
2361	hom; each block names the intended Lean declaration and its dependencies. The
2362	specialisation that closes \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is recorded in
2363	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}; an alternative descent-theoretic route is noted in
2364	\cref{rem:dual_via_stack}.
2365	
2366	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
2367	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
2368	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
2369	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
2370	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
2371	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
2372	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
2373	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
2374	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
2375	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
2376	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.
2377	
2378	\begin{definition}
2379	
2380	\leanok
2381	[The global-ring module on morphisms of presheaves of modules (value module)]
2382	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2383	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.homModule}
2384	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2385	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2386	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2387	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3508--L3514).
2388	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2389	  % "abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2390	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2391	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2392	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2393	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2394	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2395	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2396	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2397	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
2398	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
2399	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
2400	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
2401	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2402	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
2403	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
2404	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
2405	  \[
2406	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
2407	  \]
2408	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
2409	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
2410	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
2411	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
2412	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
2413	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
2414	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
2415	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
2416	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
2417	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
2418	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
2419	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
2420	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
2421	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
2422	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
2423	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
2424	\end{definition}
2425	
2426	\begin{definition}
2427	
2428	\leanok
2429	[Slice specialisation of the morphism module]
2430	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2431	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHomObjModule}
2432	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2433	  Specialise \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} to a slice category. For an object
2434	  (open) \(U\) of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\), restricting along the forgetful functor
2435	  \(\mathtt{Over.forget}\,U : \mathcal{C}/U \to \mathcal{C}\) --- the open-immersion
2436	  restriction \(M \mapsto M|_U\) --- carries \(M, N\) to presheaves of modules
2437	  \(M|_U, N|_U\) over the restricted structure ring on the slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\). The
2438	  slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\) has the terminal object \(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(\mathrm{id}_U)\),
2439	  at which the restricted ring takes the value \(R(U)\); hence
2440	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} makes the morphism group
2441	  \(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\) an \(R(U)\)-module, the
2442	  \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\). This \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) is exactly
2443	  the value \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2444	\end{definition}
2445	
2446	\begin{definition}
2447	
2448	\leanok
2449	[Presheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
2450	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2451	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHom}
2452	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:presheaf_internal_hom_value,
2453	        def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2454	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2455	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2456	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3500--L3524).
2457	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2458	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2459	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2460	  % Consider the rule
2461	  % $$
2462	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2463	  % $$
2464	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2465	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2466	  % abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2467	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2468	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2469	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2470	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2471	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2472	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2473	  % We will denote this sheaf
2474	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
2475	  % There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2476	  % $$
2477	  % \mathcal{F}
2478	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2479	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2480	  % \longrightarrow
2481	  % \mathcal{G}.
2482	  % $$"
2483	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2484	  (tag area 01CM).}
2485	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
2486	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
2487	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
2488	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
2489	  \[
2490	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
2491	      \;:=\;
2492	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
2493	  \]
2494	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
2495	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
2496	  This value module is the slice specialisation
2497	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
2498	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
2499	
2500	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
2501	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
2502	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
2503	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
2504	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
2505	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
2506	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
2507	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
2508	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
2509	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
2510	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
2511	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
2512	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
2513	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
2514	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
2515	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
2516	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
2517	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
2518	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
2519	
2520	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
2521	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
2522	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
2523	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
2524	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
2525	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
2526	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
2527	\end{definition}

2529	\begin{lemma}
2530	
2531	\leanok
2532	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
2533	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2534	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
2535	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2536	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2537	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2538	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
2539	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2540	  % "Consider the rule
2541	  % $$
2542	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2543	  % $$
2544	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2545	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2546	  % abelian groups."
2547	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2548	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
2549	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
2550	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
2551	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
2552	  is a restriction map
2553	  \[
2554	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
2555	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
2556	  \]
2557	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
2558	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
2559	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
2560	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
2561	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
2562	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
2563	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
2564	  \[
2565	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
2566	  \]
2567	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
2568	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
2569	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
2570	\end{lemma}
2571	
2572	\begin{proof}
2573	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2574	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
2575	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
2576	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
2577	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
2578	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
2579	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
2580	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
2581	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
2582	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
2583	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
2584	\end{proof}

2586	\begin{definition}
2587	
2588	\leanok
2589	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
2590	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
2591	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}
2592	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2593	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as in \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}, the
2594	  \emph{presheaf dual} of \(M\) is the internal hom into the unit (structure)
2595	  presheaf,
2596	  \[
2597	    M^\vee \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om(M, R),
2598	  \]
2599	  i.e.\ \(M^\vee(U) = \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\,(R(U))\,(M|_U \to R|_U)\), the
2600	  \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\). This is the
2601	  presheaf-of-modules analogue of the linear dual
2602	  \(\mathtt{Module.Dual}\,R\,M = (M \to R)\) that serves as the \(\otimes\)-inverse of
2603	  an invertible module over a fixed ring; here the fixed-ring dual is taken
2604	  open-by-open and assembled by the slice presentation of
2605	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2606	\end{definition}
2607	
2608	\begin{lemma}
2609	
2610	\leanok
2611	[Evaluation/contraction of the internal hom]
2612	  \label{lem:internal_hom_eval}
2613	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval}
2614	  % NOTE (iter-224): \texttt{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval} is CLOSED axiom-clean
2615	  % (\(\{\texttt{propext}, \texttt{Classical.choice}, \texttt{Quot.sound}\}\)). The full
2616	  % morphism assembles the per-open contraction \texttt{internalHomEvalApp}
2617	  % (the \(R(U)\)-bilinear \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\),
2618	  % \(s \otimes \varphi \mapsto \varphi(s)\)) sectionwise; naturality follows from the
2619	  % evaluate/restrict-commutation argument in the proof body. The iter-222/223 ``whnf
2620	  % heartbeat-bomb'' obstacle was a stale Mathlib-version artifact and is gone.
2621	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2622	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2623	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2624	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3517--L3524).
2625	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2626	  % "There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2627	  % $$
2628	  % \mathcal{F}
2629	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2630	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2631	  % \longrightarrow
2632	  % \mathcal{G}.
2633	  % $$"
2634	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2635	  (tag area 01CM).}
2636	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
2637	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2638	  \[
2639	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
2640	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
2641	  \]
2642	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
2643	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
2644	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
2645	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
2646	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
2647	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
2648	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
2649	\end{lemma}
2650	
2651	\begin{proof}
2652	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2653	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
2654	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
2655	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
2656	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
2657	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
2658	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
2659	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
2660	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
2661	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules.
2662	  Concretely, the morphism is defined by specifying, on each open \(U\), the per-open
2663	  contraction \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\).
2664	  The single remaining datum is the naturality square, which reduces to the
2665	  evaluate/restrict-commutation identity \(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\) above: the
2666	  restriction \(\phi|_V\) of a functional inside the dual \(M^\vee\) is its restriction
2667	  to the open \(V\), and restriction is functorial, so evaluating the restricted
2668	  functional on the restricted section agrees with restricting the value of the
2669	  evaluation. This is the same restriction-functoriality argument already used to make
2670	  the internal-hom restriction maps functorial in
2671	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}. This is the counit of the tensor--hom
2672	  adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} specialised to the unit presheaf.
2673	\end{proof}
2674	
2675	\begin{lemma}
2676	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
2677	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
2678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
2679	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2680	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2681	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2682	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
2683	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2684	  % "Consider the rule
2685	  % $$
2686	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2687	  % $$
2688	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2689	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2690	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2691	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2692	  (tag area 01CM).}
2693	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
2694	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
2695	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2696	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
2697	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
2698	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
2699	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
2700	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
2701	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
2702	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
2703	  \[
2704	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
2705	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
2706	  \]
2707	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
2708	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
2709	\end{lemma}
2710	
2711	\begin{proof}
2712	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2713	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
2714	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
2715	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
2716	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
2717	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
2718	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
2719	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
2720	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
2721	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
2722	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
2723	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
2724	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
2725	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
2726	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
2727	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
2728	\end{proof}
2729	
2730	\begin{lemma}
2731	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]
2732	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
2733	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
2734	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2735	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2736	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
2737	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
2738	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2739	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
2740	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
2741	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2742	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
2743	  % is an isomorphism."
2744	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
2745	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
2746	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
2747	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
2748	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
2749	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
2750	\end{lemma}
2751	
2752	\begin{proof}
2753	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2754	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
2755	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes with
2756	  open-immersion restriction --- the slice formula
2757	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} is defined through \(L|_U\), so
2758	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)\),
2759	  the restriction-compatibility being the dual analogue of
2760	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} for the internal hom. Under the trivialisation,
2761	  \[
2762	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U
2763	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
2764	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,
2765	  \]
2766	  the last identification being evaluation at \(1\), the canonical iso of the dual of
2767	  a free rank-one module with itself. Hence \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of
2768	  rank one. (Equivalently, this is item~2 of Stacks
2769	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}, tag 01CR: the dual of an invertible
2770	  module is invertible.)
2771	\end{proof}
2772	
2773	\begin{remark}
2774	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
2775	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
2776	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
2777	        lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
2778	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,lem:internal_hom_eval,%
2779	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
2780	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing
2781	  object: take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which
2782	  is a line bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, and take \(\varepsilon_L\) to
2783	  be the descended evaluation of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}. On a trivialising open
2784	  \(U\), the restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
2785	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\) and
2786	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) to the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to
2787	  \mathcal{O}_U\) of the free rank-one module with its dual, which is the left unitor
2788	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and hence an isomorphism. As ``being an isomorphism''
2789	  is local on \(X\), the global \(\varepsilon_L\) is an isomorphism
2790	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\) by
2791	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} together with the structure-sheaf-unit comparison
2792	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}. This is the realization the
2793	  ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points
2794	  to.
2795	\end{remark}
2796	
2797	\begin{remark}
2798	[Alternative route via object-level descent (fallback)]
2799	  \label{rem:dual_via_stack}
2800	  Should the slice-and-sheafify route of
2801	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} bottom out, the dual can
2802	  instead be assembled by \emph{object-level descent}: glue the local trivial duals
2803	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) along the inverse-transpose transition isomorphisms
2804	  \(g_{ij}^{-\mathsf{T}}\) of \(L\) into a global \(L^{-1}\) on \(X\). Abstractly this
2805	  is effective descent for sheaves of modules on the small Zariski site --- the
2806	  essential-surjectivity of the descent-data functor of an \(\mathtt{IsStack}\)
2807	  pseudofunctor \(U \mapsto (\mathcal{O}_U\text{-modules})\). This is the more
2808	  principled and reusable construction but the larger build (it requires constructing
2809	  the restriction pseudofunctor and proving the stack/effective-descent property),
2810	  and is recorded only as a fallback; the slice internal-hom route above is the
2811	  intended one. It is the same morphism-/object-level descent family on which the
2812	  associator re-route of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} also waits.
2813	\end{remark}

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\)
11	% as a functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). This chapter is gated on
12	% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
13	% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
14	% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a sheaf), A.2.*
15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
26	% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}
87

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}
195	
196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as
246	% the direct application `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`,
247	% axiom-clean modulo the carrier-level simplification on `OnProduct`. The
248	% invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH: pullback of invertible is
249	% invertible) is deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an
250	% invertibility witness.
251	
252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}
321	
322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}

402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}
