481	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
482	
483	The Lean target file is the new
484	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
485	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
486	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
487	Lean targets as follows.
488	
489	\begin{itemize}
490	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
491	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
492	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on \(C \times_k T\). Mathlib provides
493	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
494	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
495	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
496	    the product scheme.
497	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
498	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
499	    groups induced by \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\). The Mathlib backbone is
500	    the pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
501	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
502	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
503	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
504	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
505	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
506	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
507	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
508	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
509	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
510	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
511	    structural existence of the quotient set
512	    \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\). Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
513	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
514	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
515	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
516	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
517	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
518	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\). The naturality square
519	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
520	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
521	    quotient-induced map.
522	\end{itemize}

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94	
95	% The four conclusions of Kleiman \S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) are split below
96	% into per-conclusion theorem blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin to
97	% a Lean declaration. The verbatim source quote of lem:agps~(3) is
98	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} below
99	% (covering all four conclusions); the three subsequent blocks reference
100	% that quote rather than duplicating it. The combined proof, sharing the
101	% \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment from Kleiman's proof, follows
102	% the fourth block.
103	
104	\begin{theorem}\leanok
105	[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
106	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
108	
109	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);

208	\begin{lemma}
209	[Stacks 037Q iff-direction: connected + section ⟹ geometrically connected]
210	  \label{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
212	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], tag 037Q (criteria for geometric connectedness via
213	  % algebraic closure of the base field in the global sections) +
214	  % tag 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE: Stacks 037Q (Lemma): "Let \(X\) be a scheme over the
216	  % field \(k\). Set \(K = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The following are
217	  % equivalent: (1) \(X\) is geometrically connected over \(k\), (2)
218	  % \(X\) is connected and \(k\) is algebraically closed in \(K\), (3)
219	  % \(K\) is a field and the inclusion \(k \subset K\) is purely
220	  % inseparable."  (read from references/stacks-varieties.tex section
221	  % \S geom-connected, tag 037Q).
222	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)).}
223	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(f : X \to \Spec k\) be a morphism of
224	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
225	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
226	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
227	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
228	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
229	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is
230	  connected.
231	\end{lemma}
232	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By
233	% Lemma 04KV, it suffices to show that \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
234	% for every finite separable field extension \(k'/k\).  By Lemma 0359
235	% \(X \times_k k' = \Spec(k') \times_{\Spec k} X\) and \(H^0(X
236	% \times_k k', \mathcal{O}) = K \otimes_k k'\).  Since \(k\) is
237	% algebraically closed in \(K\), the ring \(K \otimes_k k'\) is a
238	% field (a Henselian-type criterion); a scheme whose global section
239	% ring is a field is connected.''
240	\begin{proof}
241	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
242	  Following Stacks 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)) combined
243	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
244	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
245	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
246	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
247	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
248	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
249	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
250	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
251	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
252	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along
253	  the natural map \(X \times_k k' \to X\) gives a corresponding clopen
254	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)
255	  are disjoint clopens in \(|X|\) by surjectivity of \(\Spec k' \to \Spec
256	  k\)). Since \(|X|\) is connected by hypothesis, one of those images is
257	  empty, contradicting non-triviality. The base change \(X \times_k k'\)
258	  is therefore connected.
259	
260	  Spelled out: this lemma is the iter-194 first-class formalisation of
261	  the Stacks 037Q iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1) specialised to the
262	  presence of a \(k\)-rational section (which automatically supplies
263	  the \enquote{algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(K\) equals \(k\)}
264	  condition via the section's pullback on global sections — see
265	  Stacks 037R for that step).
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	% Note: the lemma above is the substrate needed for
269	% thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes via the route described
270	% in the proof block below. iter-194+ prover work closes its body.
271	
272	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
273	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
274	% (3).)
275	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
276	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
277	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
278	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
279	% \(G^0\) is open too.
280	%
281	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
282	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
283	% extending \(k\).
284	%
285	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
286	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
287	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
288	%
289	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
290	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
291	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
292	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
293	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
294	% [\dots]
295	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
296	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
297	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
298	% [\dots]
299	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
300	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
301	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
302	\begin{proof}
303	  We establish
304	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
305	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
306	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
307	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
308	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
309	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
310	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
311	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
312	
313	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
314	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
315	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
316	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
317	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
318	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
319	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
320	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
321	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
322	
323	  \emph{Base-change commutation
324	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
325	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
326	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
327	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
328	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
329	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
330	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
331	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
332	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
333	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
334	
335	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
336	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
337	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
338	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
339	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
340	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
341	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
342	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
343	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
344	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
345	
346	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
347	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
348	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
349	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
350	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
351	  \(\overline k\), the product
352	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
353	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
354	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
355	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
356	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
357	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
358	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
359	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
360	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
361	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
362	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
363	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
364	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
368	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
369	
370	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
371	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
372	
373	\begin{definition}\leanok
374	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
375	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
377	  
378	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
379	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
380	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
381	  % L2921-L2929).
382	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
383	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
384	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
385	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
386	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
387	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
388	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
389	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
390	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
391	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
392	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
393	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
394	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
395	  % \(k\).
396	  %  \end{prp}"
397	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
398	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
399	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
400	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
401	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
402	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
403	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
404	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
405	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
406	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
407	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
408	  extension of the base field.
409	\end{definition}
410	
411	\section{The degree map}
412	\label{sec:pic_degree}
413	
414	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
415	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
416	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
417	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
418	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
419	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
420	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
421	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
422	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
423	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
424	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
425	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
426	degree.
427	
428	\begin{definition}\leanok
429	[Degree of a Picard class]
430	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
432	  
433	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
434	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
435	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
436	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
437	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
438	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
439	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
440	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
441	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
442	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
443	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
444	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
445	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
446	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
447	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
448	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
449	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
450	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
451	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
452	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
453	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
454	  \[
455	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
456	  \]
457	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
458	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
459	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
460	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
461	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
462	  \[
463	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
464	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
465	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
466	  \]
467	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
468	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
469	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
470	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
471	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
472	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
473	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
474	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
475	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
476	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
477	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
478	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
479	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
480	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
481	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
482	  of degree \(d\).
483	\end{definition}
484	
485	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
486	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
487	
488	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
489	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
490	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
491	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
492	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
493	automatically.
494	
495	% The three conclusions of the original ``\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian
496	% variety of dimension \(g\)'' theorem are split below into per-conclusion
497	% blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin. The Kleiman ex:jac + rmk:Jac
498	% verbatim source quote (covering the abelian-variety structure) is
499	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}; the Milne
500	% III.1.4(e) verbatim quote (covering the dimension formula) is at
501	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}; the Milne III.1 quote
502	% characterising \(\Pic^0(C)\) as degree-zero classes is already reproduced
503	% in \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} and referenced from
504	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. The combined proof,
505	% sharing the \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third
506	% block.
507	
508	\begin{theorem}\leanok
509	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
510	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
512	
513	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
514	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
515	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
516	  % L3990-L3996).
517	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
518	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
519	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
520	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
521	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
522	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
523	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
524	  % \end{ex}"
525	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
526	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
527	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
528	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
529	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
530	  % Exercise~REF."
531	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
532	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
533	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
535	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of
536	  \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an \emph{abelian variety over
537	  \(k\)}: it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a
538	  \(k\)-scheme, and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure. (In the
539	  Lean encoding this is the conjunction of
540	  \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]}, and
541	  \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
542	  \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with the existence of a
543	  \texttt{[GrpObj]} structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). Commutativity
544	  follows automatically from Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4 and is not
545	  separately stated.)
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{theorem}
549	
550	\leanok
551	[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]
552	  \label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
554	
555	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
556	  % \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
557	  % PDF p.~92).
558	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
559	  % "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
560	  % then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
561	  % when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
562	  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
563	  % from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
564	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
565	  \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86; cf.\ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',
566	  \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac.}
567	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
568	  \(g = g(C)\) (\cref{def:genus}) over a field \(k\). The dimension of
569	  the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
570	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:
571	  \[
572	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g(C).
573	  \]
574	\end{theorem}
575	
576	\begin{theorem}
577	
578	\leanok
579	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
580	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
582	
583	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
584	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
585	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
586	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
587	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
588	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
589	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
590	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
591	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
592	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
593	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
594	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
595	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
596	  if and only if the degree
597	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
598	  vanishes. Equivalently,
599	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
600	\end{theorem}
601	
602	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
603	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
604	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
605	% identity-component substrate.)
606	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
607	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
608	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
609	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
610	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
611	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
612	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
613	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
614	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
615	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
616	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
617	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
618	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
619	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
620	% everywhere."
621	\begin{proof}
622	  We establish
623	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
624	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
625	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
626	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
627	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
628	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
629	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
630	
631	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
632	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
633	  conjuncts from four inputs.
634	
635	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
636	  By
637	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
638	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
639	  type by
640	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
641	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
642	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
643	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
644	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
645	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
646	
647	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
648	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
649	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
650	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
651	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
652	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
653	
654	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
655	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
656	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
657	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
658	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
659	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
660	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
661	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
662	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
663	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
664	
665	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
666	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
667	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
668	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
669	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
670	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
671	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
672	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
673	
674	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
675	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
676	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
677	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
678	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
679	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
680	
681	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
682	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
683	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
684	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
685	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
686	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
687	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
688	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
689	  the open identity component, and
690	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
691	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
692	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
693	
694	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
695	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
696	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
697	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
698	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
699	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
700	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
701	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
702	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
703	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
704	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
705	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
706	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
707	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
708	  zero on \(C\)'').
709	\end{proof}
710	
711	\section{Lean encoding}
712	\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}
713	
714	The Lean target file is
715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
716	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
717	(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
718	of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
719	
720	\begin{itemize}
721	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
722	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
723	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
724	    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
725	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
726	    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
727	    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
728	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
729	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) packages the
730	    clopen-subscheme inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (open immersion
731	    \emph{and} closed immersion) --- conclusion (a) of Kleiman~\S 5
732	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
733	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
734	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
735	    the group-homomorphism property of the inclusion of
736	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: \(G^0\) inherits a
737	    \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\) compatible with the
738	    inclusion. Conclusion (b) of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
739	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
740	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible})
741	    packages the finite-type-ness and geometric-irreducibility
742	    conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (conclusion (c)).
743	    In Lean these are the instances
744	    \texttt{[LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom]},
745	    \texttt{[QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom]}, and
746	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom]}.
747	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
748	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}) packages the
749	    base-change-commutation conclusion (d) of Kleiman~\S 5
750	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3): for any field extension \(K/k\), the natural
751	    map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) (in Lean,
752	    \texttt{(IdentityComponent G)\_K \(\to\) IdentityComponent (G\_K)})
753	    is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
754	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
755	    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
756	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\) obtained by applying the
757	    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
758	    \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), inheriting the \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
759	    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
760	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
761	    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
762	    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
763	    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
764	    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
765	    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
766	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
767	    component.
768	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
769	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
770	    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
771	    conjunction \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]},
772	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
773	    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
774	    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
775	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
776	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
777	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
778	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
779	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
780	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
781	    underlying scheme).
782	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
783	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
784	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
785	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
786	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
787	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
788	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
789	\end{itemize}
790	
791	The abstract identity-component substrate
792	(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} and its four
793	conclusion-specific theorems
794	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme},
795	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism},
796	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible},
797	\texttt{IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}) is new project material: it
798	is not currently in Mathlib, and the Lean skeleton is owed in a
799	follow-up iteration. The downstream
800	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety},
801	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.finrank\_eq\_genus}, and
802	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.kPoints\_iff\_kerDegree} consume Kleiman~\S 5
803	Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac and Milne~\S III.1, both of which currently
804	live only at the blueprint level; their Lean specialisation to the
805	smooth-proper-curve case is the main substantive step of A.3.

807	\section{Out of scope}
808	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
809	
810	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
811	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
812	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
813	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
814	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
815	
816	\begin{itemize}
817	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
818	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
819	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
820	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
821	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
822	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
823	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
824	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
825	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
826	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
827	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
828	    Route~A.
829	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
830	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
831	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
832	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
833	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
834	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
835	\end{itemize}

27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
41	
42	\begin{theorem}\leanok
43	[Tangent space at the identity: \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)]
44	  \label{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}
45	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.tangentSpaceIso}
46	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
47	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
48	        def:genus}
49	
50	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~thm:tgtsp;
51	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~41 (read from
52	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3265-L3270).
53	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
54	  % "\begin{thm}\label{thm:tgtsp}
55	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
56	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Let \(\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\) denote the
57	  % tangent space at \(0\).  Then
58	  %    \(\)\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}=\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
59	  %  \end{thm}"
60	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
61	  Thm.~thm:tgtsp; cf.\ [Hartshorne], ``Algebraic Geometry'', GTM 52,
62	  IV.1.1 (genus def.\ \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)).}
63	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
64	  \(g = g(C)\) over a field \(k\), and let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
65	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) be the identity component of the
66	  Picard scheme of \(C/k\). There is a canonical isomorphism of
67	  \(k\)-vector spaces
68	  \[
69	    \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \;\cong\; H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C).
70	  \]
71	  In particular, the \(k\)-dimension of \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals
72	  the genus \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)
73	  (\cref{def:genus}).
74	\end{theorem}
75	
76	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
77	% L3358-L3367 + L3404-L3407: the construction of the comparison map and
78	% its identification as an isomorphism. Kleiman's full proof spans
79	% L3271-L3408 and is too long for one verbatim block, so we reproduce
80	% the two paragraphs that contain the load-bearing exact sequence and
81	% the conclusion; see Notes for Plan Agent.)
82	% "To compute this kernel, set \(X_\ve:=X\ox_kk_\ve\), and form the truncated
83	% exponential sequence of sheaves of Abelian groups:
84	%    \(\)0\to \mc O_{\!X} \to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^* \to \mc O_{\!X}^* \to 1,\(\)
85	%  where the first map takes a local section \(b\) to \(1+b\ve\).  This
86	% sequence is split by the map \(\mc O_{\!X}^*\to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*\) defined by
87	% \(a\mapsto a+0\cdot\ve\).  Hence taking cohomology yields this split exact
88	% sequence of Abelian groups:
89	%    \(\)0\to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}) \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*)
90	%     \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}^*) \to 1.\(\)"
91	% "In Square (\ref{CD:tgtsp}), the two vertical maps are isomorphisms by
92	% Exercise~\ref{ex:Alr} since \(\IPic_{X/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\) and
93	% \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, \(v\)
94	% too is an isomorphism, as desired."
95	\begin{proof}
96	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
97	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
98	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~thm:tgtsp. By
99	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
100	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
101	  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
102	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
103	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
104	  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
105	  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.
106	
107	  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
108	  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
109	  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
110	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
111	  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
112	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
113	  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). When \(P\) is a \(k\)-group scheme,
114	  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
115	  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
116	  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
117	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
118	  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
119	  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
120	  \[
121	    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
122	    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
123	  \]
124	
125	  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
126	  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
127	  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
128	  abelian groups
129	  \[
130	    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
131	        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
132	  \]
133	  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
134	  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
135	  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
136	  \[
137	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
138	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
139	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
140	  \]
141	
142	  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
143	  represents the étale-sheafification of
144	  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
145	  commutative diagram of abelian groups
146	  \[
147	    \begin{array}{ccc}
148	      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
149	        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
150	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
151	      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
152	        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
153	    \end{array}
154	  \]
155	  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
156	  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 5, Exercise~ex:Alr;
157	  invocation legitimised by
158	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
159	  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
160	  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
161	  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
162	  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
163	  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
164	  detected after a faithfully flat extension).
165	
166	  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
167	  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
168	  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
169	  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
170	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
171	  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
172	  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.
173	
174	  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
175	  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
176	  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
177	\end{proof}
178	
179	\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
180	\label{sec:pic0_smooth}
181	
182	By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
183	\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
184	equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
185	smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
186	at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
187	translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
188	dimension \(g\).
189	
190	\begin{theorem}\leanok
191	[Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
192	  \label{thm:pic0_smooth}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.smooth}
194	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
195	        def:pic_zero_subscheme,
196	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
197	
198	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0;
199	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~44 (read from
200	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3427 +
201	  % L3442-L3446).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
204	  % \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
205	  %    \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
206	  %  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
207	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
208	  % everywhere.
209	  %  \end{cor}"
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:ch0}
212	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
213	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\), then
214	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere.
215	  %  \end{cor}"
216	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
217	  Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties''
218	  (course notes, 2008), \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (dimension of \(J\)
219	  equals the genus).}
220	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
221	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
222	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is smooth over \(k\) of dimension
223	  \(g\): the structural morphism
224	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is smooth, and every closed point of
225	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) lies in a smooth open neighbourhood.
226	\end{theorem}
227	
228	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
229	% L3428-L3439: the proof of Cor.~cor:sm. We reproduce the full proof,
230	% which deduces smoothness everywhere from smoothness at \(0\) by
231	% translation.)
232	% "\begin{proof} Plainly we may assume \(k\) is algebraically closed. Then,
233	% given any closed point \(\lambda\in\IPic_{X/k}\), there is an automorphism
234	% of \(\IPic_{X/k}\) that carries 0 to \(\lambda\), namely, ``multiplication''
235	% by \(\lambda\).  So \(\IPic_{X/k}\) has the same dimension at \(\lambda\) as
236	% at 0, and \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is
237	% smooth at 0.
238	%
239	% By general principles, \(\dim_0 \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\),
240	% and equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\).
241	% Moreover, \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\) if and only if it is smooth
242	% at \(0\) since \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, the corollary
243	% results from Theorem~\ref{thm:tgtsp}.
244	%  \end{proof}"
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
247	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
248	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
249	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:sm, with the curve-specific dimension
250	  input supplied by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}.
251	
252	  Since smoothness of a scheme over \(k\) descends along faithfully
253	  flat extensions, we may replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure
254	  \(\bar k\) (using \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
255	  to identify \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\)).
256	  Over \(\bar k\) every closed point of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
257	  \(\bar k\)-rational, and the group law translates the identity \(0\)
258	  onto an arbitrary closed point: the translation morphism
259	  \(t_\lambda : \Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(x \mapsto x +
260	  \lambda\), is an automorphism by
261	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}. Consequently
262	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) has the same local dimension at \(\lambda\) as at
263	  \(0\), and is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is smooth at
264	  \(0\).
265	
266	  By the standard tangent-space dimension bound, for any Noetherian
267	  local ring \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) with residue field \(k\) one has
268	  \(\dim A \le \dim_k (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)\), with equality
269	  if and only if \(A\) is regular. Over an algebraically closed field,
270	  regularity at a \(\bar k\)-rational point is equivalent to
271	  smoothness. Hence
272	  \(
273	    \dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)
274	  \)
275	  by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}, with equality iff
276	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular (equivalently, smooth) at \(0\).
277	
278	  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
279	  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
280	  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~ex:jac
281	  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
282	  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
283	  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
284	  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
285	  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:ch0): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
286	  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
287	  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
288	  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
289	  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
290	  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
291	  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
292	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
293	  above, it is smooth everywhere.
294	
295	  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
296	  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
297	\end{proof}
298	
299	\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
300	\label{sec:pic0_proper}
301	
302	A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
303	so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
304	quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
305	projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
306	
307	\begin{theorem}\leanok
308	[Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
309	  \label{thm:pic0_proper}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper}
311	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
312	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
313	
314	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p;
315	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
316	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2940).
317	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
318	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
319	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
320	  % and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
321	  % quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
322	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective.
323	  %  \end{thm}"
324	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
325	  Thm.~th:qpp\&p; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes,
326	  2008), \S I.6 (abelian varieties are projective, p.~27).}
327	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
328	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
329	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is projective over \(k\), in
330	  particular proper: the structural morphism
331	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is a projective morphism, hence
332	  satisfies the universal closedness and separatedness conditions of
333	  properness.
334	\end{theorem}
335	
336	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
337	% L2941-L2984: the proof of th:qpp&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
338	% structure theorem to reduce projectivity to ruling out non-constant
339	% maps from the multiplicative group; the curve case relies on
340	% geometric normality of \(C\).)
341	% "Suppose \(X\) is also geometrically normal.  Since \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is
342	% quasi-projective, to prove it is projective, it suffices to prove it is
343	% proper.  By Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}, forming \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) commutes
344	% with extending \(k\).  And by \cite[2.7.1(vii)]{EGAIV2}, a \(k\)-scheme is
345	% complete if (and only if) it is after extending \(k\).  So we may, and do,
346	% assume \(k\) is algebraically closed.
347	%
348	% Recall the structure theorem of Chevalley and Rosenlicht for algebraic
349	% groups, or reduced connected group schemes of finite type over \(k\); see
350	% \cite[Thm.~1.1, p.~3]{Co02}.  The theorem says that every algebraic
351	% group is an extension of an Abelian variety (or complete algebraic
352	% group) by a linear (or affine) algebraic group.  Recall also that every
353	% solvable linear algebraic \(k\)-group is triangularizable (the
354	% Lie--Kolchin theorem); so, if it's nontrivial, then it contains a copy
355	% of the multiplicative group or of the additive group; see \cite[(10.5)
356	% and (10.2)]{Bo69}.  Now,  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is commutative, so solvable.
357	% Hence it suffices to show that, if \(T\) denotes the
358	% affine line minus the origin, then every \(k\)-map \(t\:T\to
359	% (\IPicz_{X/k})_{\red}\) is constant."
360	\begin{proof}
361	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
362	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
363	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
364	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, specialised to \(X = C\) a
365	  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
366	
367	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
368	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
369	  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
370	  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
371	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
372	  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
373	  (\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, first conclusion) then gives that
374	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
375	  is projective and geometrically integral.
376	
377	  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
378	  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
379	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
380	  commutes with extension of \(k\)
381	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
382	  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
383	  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
384	  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.
385	
386	  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
387	  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
388	  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
389	  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
390	  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
391	  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
392	  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
393	  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
394	  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
395	  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
396	  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
397	  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
398	  is constant.
399	
400	  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
401	  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
402	  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
403	  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
404	  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
405	  theorem (Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:cmp) \(t\) is represented by an
406	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
407	  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
408	  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
409	  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
410	  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
411	  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
412	  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
413	  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
414	  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
415	  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
416	  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
417	  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
418	  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
419	  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
420	  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
421	  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
422	  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
423	  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
424	  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
425	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
426	  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
427	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
428	  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
429	  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
430	  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
431	  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
432	  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
433	  properness.
434	\end{proof}
435	
436	\section{Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
437	\label{sec:pic0_geom_irred}
438	
439	Geometric irreducibility is delivered directly by the abstract
440	identity-component substrate
441	(\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}) applied
442	to the \(k\)-group scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\). We restate the
443	specialisation here for the assembly downstream.
444	
445	\begin{theorem}\leanok
446	[Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
447	  \label{thm:pic0_geom_irred}
448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible}
449	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
450	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
451	
452	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0;
453	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
454	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2921-L2929).
455	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
456	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
457	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
458	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
459	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
460	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
461	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
462	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
463	  % \(k\).
464	  %  \end{prp}"
465	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0
466	  (geometric irreducibility of the connected component of the
467	  identity).}
468	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
469	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
470	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is geometrically irreducible: after
471	  base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the scheme
472	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) is irreducible.
473	\end{theorem}
474	
475	\begin{proof}
476	  \uses{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
477	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes,
478	        thm:fga_pic_representability}
479	  Apply the abstract identity-component substrate to the \(k\)-group
480	  scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), which is locally of finite type by
481	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. By
482	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}, the
483	  identity component \((\Pic_{C/k})^0 = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite
484	  type and geometrically irreducible over \(k\). The base-change
485	  compatibility of
486	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes} identifies
487	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\),
488	  closing the proof.
489	\end{proof}
490	
491	\section{Assembly: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety}
492	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
493	
494	A \(k\)-group scheme is an \emph{abelian variety} iff its underlying
495	\(k\)-scheme is a complete (proper) connected group variety
496	(Milne~\S I.1, p.~8). Combining the smoothness, properness, and
497	geometric irreducibility statements of this chapter with the
498	identity-component group-scheme structure of
499	\cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} packages \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an
500	abelian variety of dimension \(g\). This is the load-bearing A.3.vii
501	gate for Route~A.
502	
503	\begin{theorem}\leanok
504	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
505	  \label{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety}
507	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
508	        thm:pic0_proper,
509	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
510	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
511	
512	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
513	  % \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety) (read from
514	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~14).
515	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
516	  % "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian variety. As we
517	  % shall see, they are projective, and (fortunately) commutative. Their
518	  % group laws will be written additively. Thus \(t_a\) is now denoted
519	  % \(x \mapsto x + a\) and \(e\) is usually denoted \(0\)."
520	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
521	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~51 (read from
522	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3990-L3996).
523	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
524	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
525	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
526	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
527	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
528	  % Exercise~REF."
529	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
530	  \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], ``The
531	  Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac (the Jacobian
532	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a
533	  projective abelian \(S\)-scheme).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
535	  genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component
536	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is an
537	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)} of dimension \(g\): the underlying
538	  \(k\)-scheme is smooth (\cref{thm:pic0_smooth}), proper
539	  (\cref{thm:pic0_proper}), and geometrically irreducible
540	  (\cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme
541	  structure inherited from \(\Pic_{C/k}\) via the inclusion
542	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
543	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}). In
544	  particular, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne~\S
545	  I.1, Cor.~1.4).
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{proof}
549	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
550	        thm:pic0_proper,
551	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
552	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
553	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
554	  We assemble Milne's defining axioms of an abelian variety from the
555	  four conjuncts established earlier in this chapter and in
556	  \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}.
557	
558	  \emph{(i) Group-variety structure.} By
559	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} applied to
560	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
561	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}), the identity component
562	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
563	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) compatible with the clopen inclusion.
564	
565	  \emph{(ii) Smoothness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}, the structural
566	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth (of relative
567	  dimension \(g\)). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth
568	  \(k\)-variety: it is reduced (Milne's standing convention bundles
569	  reducedness into ``variety''), geometrically reduced (by
570	  smoothness over \(k\)), and locally of finite type.
571	
572	  \emph{(iii) Properness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_proper}, the structural
573	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is proper (in fact
574	  projective). This is Milne's ``complete'' axiom.
575	
576	  \emph{(iv) Connectedness (geometric irreducibility).} By
577	  \cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}, the underlying scheme
578	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; in particular it is
579	  geometrically connected, hence connected.
580	
581	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, proper,
582	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme equipped with a
583	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure. By Milne~\S I.1, p.~8, ``a complete
584	  connected group variety is called an abelian variety''. Thus
585	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety over \(k\); commutativity is
586	  automatic (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Its dimension is the relative
587	  dimension of the smooth morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\),
588	  which equals \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) by
589	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso} and \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}.
590	
591	  This is the content of Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac: for \(X/S\)
592	  projective and smooth with connected curve fibres of genus
593	  \(g > 0\) (specialise to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\)), the Jacobian
594	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) exists and is a projective abelian
595	  \(S\)-scheme. The genus-zero case is degenerate
596	  (\(\Pic^0_{C/k} = 0\), Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)) but is
597	  consistent with the assembly: a point scheme \(\Spec k\) is
598	  trivially an abelian variety of dimension \(0\).
599	\end{proof}

658	\section{Out of scope}
659	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety_out_of_scope}
660	
661	This chapter packages \emph{only} the five A.3.iii--vii steps. The
662	following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters
663	and are explicitly out of scope here:
664	
665	\begin{itemize}
666	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
667	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
668	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
669	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}.
670	  \item \textbf{Autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
671	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6; Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac, Abel map) ---
672	    not needed for the Jacobian's existence as an abelian variety,
673	    hence out of scope.
674	  \item \textbf{Torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
675	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
676	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
677	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
678	    Route~A.
679	  \item \textbf{Genus-zero degenerate case.} When \(g(C) = 0\),
680	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the trivial group scheme \(\Spec k\)
681	    (Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)); this is consistent with the
682	    abelian-variety assembly (a point is trivially an abelian variety
683	    of dimension \(0\)) and is handled uniformly here, but is not
684	    separately stated.
685	\end{itemize}

15	% through the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) -- the
16	% case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
17	% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
18	% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
19	%

21	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
22	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
23	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
24	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
25	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an

27	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability; it is a
