123	\subsection{Piece (i): sub-lemma decomposition for iter-128+ build}
124	\label{subsec:RigidityKbar_piece_i_decomposition}
125	
126	We decompose piece (i) above into three sub-steps (i.a)\,\(\to\)\,(i.b)\,\(\to\)\,(i.c) that are well-suited to staged Lean formalisation. Sub-step (i.a) defines the Lie algebra (as a \(k\)-vector space) of a group scheme at the identity and pins its rank; sub-step (i.b) globalises that fibre across \(G\) via a functorial shear isomorphism; sub-step (i.c) concludes the freeness and rank of the relative cotangent \(\Omega_{G/k}\).
127	
128	All three sub-steps of this group-scheme cotangent-triviality piece are stated over an arbitrary base field \(k\) (the cotangent triviality of \(G\) is intrinsic to the group scheme and does not need the alg-closed hypothesis; see \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot" in the chapter introduction, where the alg-closed base is required only for the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain, not here); the prose carefully avoids any reliance on \(k\)-rational or \(\bar k\)-rational point lifting, working functorially throughout. Sub-step (i.a) is the natural first target for the iter-128+ prover lane: it has the smallest signature surface (a finite-rank free \(k\)-module attached to the identity section of \(G\)), and no scheme-level globalisation.
129	
130	\paragraph{Iter-144 chart-algebra pivot --- disposition (per \texttt{mathlib-analogist-chart-algebra-iter144} + STRATEGY.md \S "Iter-144 chart-algebra pivot" block).}
131	Effective iter-145+, the M2.body-pile commits to the \textbf{chart-algebra route}. Under this commitment:
132	\begin{itemize}
133	  \item Piece (i.b) Step 2 d\_app sub-sorry inside \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj}'s proof, the IsIso theorem \cref{lem:GrpObj_basechange_along_proj_two_inv_app_isIso} (Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv\_app\_isIso}), and piece (i.b) Main \cref{lem:GrpObj_mulRight_globalises} (Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) are \textbf{DESCOPED from critical-path}. \emph{Status correction (iter-155):} \texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent} was subsequently \textbf{EXCISED from the Lean tree at iter-145}; \texttt{GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} \(+\) \texttt{shearMulRight} survive only as a \emph{design template}, \textbf{not} a present declaration. This excised gap-(i) globalisation (\(\sim 800\)--\(1500\) LOC) is exactly the gap (i) that the keystone \(\mathrm df = 0\) (chapter introduction) is gated on; the blueprint blocks below for these declarations are therefore an off-path record of the bundled-route design, not a live in-tree obligation.
134	  \item Piece (i.c.1/i.c.2/i.c.3) (\cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_free} + \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_rank_eq_dim} assembly) is \textbf{DESCOPED}. Chart-algebra routes the freeness and rank facts through chart-level \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} per-chart, not through a global sheaf-of-modules construction.
135	  \item Piece (iii) scheme-level absolute Frobenius PHANTOM (\textasciitilde\(800\)--\(1500\) LOC, \texttt{Scheme.absoluteFrobenius} ABSENT in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) is \textbf{DESCOPED}; chart-algebra uses ring-level \texttt{RingHom.iterateFrobenius\_comm} from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius} per-chart, giving Frobenius iteration as a direct \texttt{RingHom}-commutation with no scheme-level lift.
136	  \item Piece (ii) is \textbf{INFLATED} to absorb the chart-algebra (\(\alpha\)) \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout}-from-affine-product helper and (\(\beta\)) per-chart translation-invariance Kähler-derivation argument upstream; see the \S "Iter-144 chart-algebra envelope for piece (ii)" paragraph below for the updated envelope (\textasciitilde\(600\)--\(1050\) LOC total).
137	\end{itemize}
138	
139	Piece (i.a) --- \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} (\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity}) and \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} (\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_finrank\_eq}), closed iter-128 \(\to\) iter-132 --- remains in-tree as standalone infrastructure. Chart-algebra does \emph{not} consume it on the rigidity critical path, but the cotangent-space-at-the-identity definition and its rank are independently useful downstream (Lie-algebra dual \(\mathfrak g\) on the tangent side, etc.).
140	
141	\paragraph{Iter-144 chart-algebra envelope for piece (ii) (per \texttt{mathlib-analogist-chart-algebra-iter144}).}
142	Piece (ii) (scheme-level \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f\) factors through \(\Spec k\), the C.2.d / M2.a body consumer of piece (i)'s cotangent-trivial output) is the load-bearing body under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot. Total envelope \textasciitilde\(600\)--\(1050\) LOC, decomposed as:
143	\begin{itemize}
144	  \item Chart-algebra (\(\alpha\)) \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout}-from-affine-product helper: \textasciitilde\(80\)--\(150\) LOC. Constructs the \texttt{Algebra.IsPushout} instance on the chart-level ring extensions \(\Gamma(\Spec k, U) \to \Gamma(W, \mathcal O_G)\) for affine charts \(V \to W\) in the product \(G \otimes G\). Built from \texttt{pullbackSpecIso} and the \texttt{\_hom\_fst} / \texttt{\_inv\_fst} simp companions in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullbacks}, chained with \texttt{CommRingCat.isPushout\_iff\_isPushout} and \texttt{isPullback\_SpecMap\_of\_isPushout} (cf.\ \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj} Route (a) item 1).
145	  \item Chart-algebra (\(\beta\)) per-chart translation-invariance Kähler-derivation argument: \textasciitilde\(150\)--\(300\) LOC. The load-bearing core of M2.a's \(\mathrm df = 0\) derivation chain (per STRATEGY.md \S "Soundness rules: M2.a \(\mathrm df = 0\) derivation chain --- articulated (added iter-144)"). The three-layer chain is:
146	    \begin{enumerate}
147	      \item \textbf{Chart-local layer.} On each affine chart \(W = \Spec B \subseteq A.\mathrm{left}\) with affine \(V \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}\) such that \(f\) restricts to a ring map \(f^{\sharp} \colon B \to \Gamma(C, V)\), the differential \(\mathrm df = 0\) at the chart level means the composite \(\Omega_{B/k} \to \Omega_{\Gamma(C, V)/k}\) obtained from the universal Kähler derivation \(\mathrm d \colon B \to \Omega_{B/k}\) post-composed with \(f^{\sharp}\) vanishes. Combined with piece (i)'s chart-level free-of-rank-\(n\) presentation of \(\Omega_{B/k}\) (\texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential}), the kernel-extraction step gives \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}\) for every \(b \in B\), where \(p = \operatorname{char} k\) and \(e \geq 0\) is a chart-uniform Frobenius depth.
148	      \item \textbf{Char-\(p\) Frobenius-iteration layer.} The chart-local conclusion \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}\) is promoted to "for every \(b \in B\), there exists \(e\) with \(f^{\sharp}(b) = \mathrm{iterateFrobenius}_{p, e}(c_{b})\) for some \(c_{b} \in \Gamma(C, V)\)" via \texttt{RingHom.iterateFrobenius\_comm} (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius}, verified iter-144). The uniform-\(e\)-on-\(W\) glue extracts a globally-bounded Frobenius depth on \(C\) from the smoothness of \(A\) at the image of \(f\) (using \(A\)'s tangent-bundle triviality at each point in the image of \(f\) to control the depth uniformly across \(W\)). In characteristic \(0\) this layer is vacuous: \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f^{\sharp}(b) \in k \subseteq \Gamma(C, V)\) directly via the standard derivation-kernel argument (no Frobenius).
149	      \item \textbf{Constancy-from-\(\mathrm df = 0\) layer (the converse only).} \emph{Iter-155 correction.} This layer was previously titled the ``No-Serre-duality layer'' and claimed the chart-algebra (\(\beta\)) chain ``replaces'' the \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) route, ``eliminating piece (iv) as a critical-path dependency''. \textbf{That claim is false} (per \texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) and is corrected here. What the chain actually delivers is the \emph{converse} implication: \textbf{given} the chart-uniform input \(f^{\sharp}(b) \in \Gamma(C, V)^{p^{e}}\) (which is the chart-level shadow of the hypothesis \(\mathrm df = 0\)), the conclusion ``\(f\) factors through \(\Spec k\)'' follows from the integrally-closed-constants helper (next item) and the scheme-level lift via \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} (final item). It does \emph{not} produce the hypothesis \(\mathrm df = 0\), and it does not avoid the \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) input: that input is exactly what is needed to \emph{establish} \(\mathrm df = 0\) in the first place. Concretely, \(\mathrm df = 0\) is a global-sections fact --- with the gap-(i) trivialisation \(f^{*}\Omega_{A/k} \cong \mathcal O_C^{\oplus g}\), \(\mathrm df \in \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) --- and the chart-by-chart machinery cannot detect it because \(\Omega_{C/k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero) on every affine chart \(V\). Hence piece (iv) (or an equivalent yielding \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\)) is \textbf{on} the critical path for the keystone, not eliminated by this chain.
150	    \end{enumerate}
151	  \item Algebra-level core \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.mem\_range\_algebraMap\_of\_D\_eq\_zero}: \textasciitilde\(200\)--\(350\) LOC. Mathlib-shaped helper for the ring-level half of \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow f^{\sharp}\) factors through \(k\), packaged as the statement "for \(D \colon R \to M\) the universal Kähler derivation with \(D \circ f^{\sharp} = 0\), the image of \(f^{\sharp}\) lies in the integral closure of the image of the base ring". This is the chart-uniform core consumed by layer (1) of the \(\beta\)-chain.
152	  \item Integrally-closed-constants helper: \textasciitilde\(50\)--\(100\) LOC. The wrapper around "constants in \(C\) are integral over \(k\) for \(C\) smooth proper geometrically irreducible", used to promote the chart-uniform Frobenius-depth conclusion to a global \(\Spec k\)-factorisation.
153	  \item Scheme-level lift via \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}: \textasciitilde\(100\)--\(150\) LOC. Closes the argument by exhibiting a non-empty open \(U \subseteq C\) on which \(f|_{U}\) equals the constant morphism at the image of \(f\)'s chart-uniform output, then applies the iter-125-refactored \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}) to globalise.
154	\end{itemize}
155	
156	The chart-algebra route replaces the bundled scheme-level absolute-Frobenius construction (piece (iii) PHANTOM) with chart-uniform ring-level idioms already in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227}. \textbf{It does not, however, replace the Serre-duality cohomology vanishing (piece (iv))} --- the iter-155 correction above and in \cref{subsec:RigidityKbar_piece_i_decomposition} retracts that earlier claim. The chart-algebra (\(\beta\)) chain is the \emph{converse} ``\(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constant''; producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) remains a global-sections fact gated on \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = 0\) (piece (iv)) plus the gap-(i) trivialisation. The Lean target name for the scheme-level converse half is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_diff\_zero} (per the iter-126 analogist's idiom alignment); see \cref{lem:Scheme_Over_ext_of_diff_zero} for the present thin-wrapper Lean decl versus the gated substantive \(\mathrm df = \mathrm dg\) refinement.
157	
158	\paragraph{Iter-128 / iter-129 prover lane re-scoping (per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128} + iter-129 rename).} Sub-step (i.a) was originally a 2-target pair and is now (post-iter-129) a 3-target trio: \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} (the \emph{definition} of \(\mathfrak g^\vee\), plus the finitely-generated-free claim — iter-128 lane, body landed), \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} (the \emph{bridge} to the local-ring cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^{2}\) — iter-130+ build target), and \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} (the \emph{rank-equals-relative-dimension} claim — iter-130+ build target). The iter-128 prover lane attacks ONLY the first: it scaffolds the Lean declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} (renamed iter-129 from the placeholder \texttt{lieAlgebra}; signature relaxed to a free \texttt{\{n : \mathbb N\} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} binder so downstream consumers of arbitrary relative dimension can instantiate it) and closes its body. The bridge lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} is vestigial on the live path under Replacement (B) (see \S Q4 collapse footer at the rank-lemma proof's end) and remains deferred indefinitely; the rank lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} is the iter-132 prover-lane target (closure path: Step 1 chart-side Kähler rank from \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}'s existential + Step 2 base-change to \(k\) via \texttt{Module.finrank\_baseChange}, both detailed in that lemma's proof body). Rationale: (i) bundling definition + bridge + rank into one prover-iter task is borderline-too-ambitious per \texttt{progress-critic-iter128}; (ii) the rank-lemma RHS Lean encoding needs blueprint pinning iter-129 before being prover-ready (it must read \texttt{Module.finrank k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G) = n} parameterised by an explicit \texttt{[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} instance; the bare \(\dim G\) in the prose does not type-check directly because Mathlib has no \(\dim G\) function on \texttt{Over (Spec k)}, per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128} CHALLENGE on (i.a) signature ambiguity).
159	
160	Throughout this subsection, \(k\) is an arbitrary field and \(G : \Over\,(\Spec k)\) is a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\), with identity section \(\eta_G : \mathbf{1} \to G\).
161	
162	\begin{lemma}\leanok
163	[(i.a) Cotangent space at the identity of a group scheme]
164	  \label{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity}
166	  % Lean signature stub (pinned post-iter-129 fixup; relaxed from the
167	  % iter-128 hardcoded rel-dim-1 form so the same declaration serves
168	  % consumers of arbitrary relative dimension, e.g. \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
169	  % applied to an abelian variety of dimension \(g\)):
170	  %   noncomputable def cotangentSpaceAtIdentity (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
171	  %       [CategoryTheory.GrpObj G] {n : ℕ} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]
172	  %       [IsProper G.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom] : ModuleCat k
173	  The pullback along the identity section \(\eta_G\) of the relative cotangent presheaf,
174	  \[
175	    \mathfrak g^{\vee} \;:=\; \eta_G^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k},
176	  \]
177	  is a finitely generated free \(k\)-module. We call \(\mathfrak g^{\vee}\) the \emph{cotangent space of \(G\) at the identity}. The \emph{Lie algebra of \(G\)} as a \(k\)-vector space is recovered as the \(k\)-linear dual \(\mathfrak g := \mathfrak g^{\vee\vee} = \mathrm{Hom}_{k}(\mathfrak g^{\vee}, k)\) (the bracket structure is not used in the rigidity argument and is not packaged here).
178	
179	  \emph{Lean encoding note (iter-129 dualisation pinning).} The Lean declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} returns a \texttt{ModuleCat k} which is the \emph{un-dualised} cotangent \(\mathfrak g^{\vee}\) — the direct output of the pullback-along-section construction described in the proof, with no internal \texttt{Module.Dual} hop. Downstream consumers that need the tangent \(\mathfrak g\) take \texttt{Module.Dual k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G)} externally. This convention pins what was left open in the iter-128 encoding note: the rigidity argument is symmetric under dualisation (only the finite-rank-free claim is consumed), so the un-dualised form is preferred as it matches the body construction without an extra contravariant hop. The declaration embeds into Mathlib's \texttt{ModuleCat} infrastructure and supplies the \texttt{Module.Free k} / \texttt{Module.Finite k} typeclasses needed for the rank lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank} via the bridge lemma \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge}.
180	\end{lemma}
181	
182	\begin{proof}
183	
184	\leanok
185	  The identity section \(\eta_G\) is a canonical \(k\)-rational point of \(G\) supplied by the \(\GrpObj\) structure. The iter-131 Lean construction (Replacement (B), pure-term \texttt{noncomputable def}) realises \(\mathfrak g^{\vee} = \eta_G^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\) as a chart-base-changed Kähler module: by the forward Jacobian criterion \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega} (already in the tree as \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth\_locally\_free\_omega} in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean}), there exists an affine chart \(V \subseteq G\) around the image of the identity section \(\eta_G(\mathrm{pt})\), with affine base chart \(U \subseteq \Spec k\) and the structural ring map \(\Gamma(\Spec k, U) \to \Gamma(G, V)\) exhibiting \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension} \(n\) (and consequently \(\Omega_{\Gamma(G,V) \,/\, \Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}\) is a free \(\Gamma(G, V)\)-module of rank \(n\) by \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}). The cotangent space at the identity is then defined as the base change to \(k\) of this algebraic Kähler module along the ring map \(\psi_V \colon \Gamma(G, V) \to k\) obtained by restricting the identity section \(\eta_G\) to \(V\) and composing with the canonical isomorphism \(\Gamma(\Spec k, \cdot) \cong k\):
186	  \[
187	    \mathfrak g^{\vee} \;\;:=\;\; k \,\otimes_{\Gamma(G,\,V)}\, \Omega_{\Gamma(G,\,V) \,/\, \Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}.
188	  \]
189	  This is a finitely generated free \(k\)-module of rank \(n\) by base-change of a free module along \(\psi_V\) (the rank is preserved; see \cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank}).
190	
191	  \emph{Caveat on canonicity.} The construction depends on a chart choice \(V\) extracted via a \texttt{Classical.choose}-chain from the existential statement of \texttt{smooth\_locally\_free\_omega}; it is therefore not canonically attached to \(G\) in the strictest sense. (The iter-131 fix-up refactored what was previously a top-level \texttt{Classical.choice}-wrapper into a pure-term \texttt{let}-chain of \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} accessors, exposing the outer head symbol \texttt{(ModuleCat.extendScalars\,\(\psi_V\).hom).obj\,(ModuleCat.of\,\(\Gamma\)(G, V)\,\(\Omega\)[\(\Gamma\)(G, V)\,/\,\(\Gamma\)(Spec k, U)])} to downstream consumers; see \S "Iter-131 \texttt{Classical.choose}-chain body shape" below for the Lean encoding details, and the companion \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace_extendScalars_witness} for the structural-shape rewrite handle.) For the live consumer (rigidity over \(k\), via \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}), the existence of a rank-\(n\) free \(k\)-module is the only structural content needed, so canonicity is not load-bearing. A canonical (chart-independent) presentation via the stalk-side cotangent \(\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}/\mathfrak m_{\eta_G}^{2}\) is available as a future bridge (\cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangent_bridge} below, currently \texttt{\textbackslash notready}) at additional cost; the project defers that bridge until a non-rigidity consumer materialises that requires canonicity.
192	\end{proof}
193

23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:cross01_substrate_setup}
25	
26	The Lane B cocycle \texttt{gmScalingP1\_chart\_agreement\_cross01} reduces, after
27	the iter-188 (III.c) separated-locus structural setup, to a single substantive
28	factorisation: a pair morphism
29	\(s_{\text{pair}} : T \to T_0 \times_X T_1\) into a fibre product over the
30	projective line bar \(\mathrm{PLB}_{\overline{k}}\) must factor through the
31	diagonal closed immersion
32	\(\Delta : T_0 \times_{X} T_1 \hookrightarrow T_0 \times_{\overline{k}} T_1\)
33	of the separated-over-\(\overline{k}\) target. Concretely the missing primitive is
34	the equivalence
35	\[
36	  \exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g \iff \mathrm{range}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i)
37	\]
38	for a closed immersion \(i\) with reduced source/target hypothesis, which Mathlib
39	ships only in the kernel-inequality form (\(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\),
40	keyed on \(f.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)). Substrate~1 fills this gap.
41	
42	The same Lane B cocycle, together with the two off-target instances
43	\texttt{gm\_geomIrred} and \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}, also requires
44	\[
45	  \mathrm{IsDomain}\bigl(\mathrm{Away}_{\overline{k}}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\,\overline{k}\bigr)
46	\]
47	for a homogeneous generator \(f\) of positive degree in the
48	\(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading. Substrate~2 supplies this via a
49	canonical iso chain
50	\[
51	  \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \overline{k}[t]
52	  \;\cong\; \mathrm{MvPolynomial}_{\mathrm{Unit}}(\mathrm{Away}\,f)
53	\]
54	followed by localisation at \(X()\), reducing the claim to the
55	already-formalised pattern from \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}.
56	
57	\section{Closed-immersion lift by range inclusion}
58	\label{sec:lift_iff_range_subset}
59	
60	\begin{theorem}\leanok
61	[lift through closed immersion via range containment]
62	  \label{thm:IsClosedImmersion_lift_iff_range_subset}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsClosedImmersion.lift_iff_range_subset}
64	  Let \(X,Y,Z\) be schemes (Type \(u\)), let \(i : Z \to X\) be a closed
65	  immersion with \(Z\) reduced, and let \(g : Y \to X\) be a
66	  quasi-compact morphism with \(Y\) reduced. Then there exists a
67	  morphism \(h : Y \to Z\) with \(h \circ i = g\) if and only if the
68	  topological range of \(g\) is contained in the topological range of
69	  \(i\):
70	  \[
71	    (\exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g)
72	    \;\iff\;
73	    \mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}).
74	  \]
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{proof}
77	
78	
79	\leanok
80	  \emph{Forward direction} is range pushdown: if \(g = h \circ i\)
81	  then every point in \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}})\) is the image
82	  under \(i_{\text{base}}\) of \(h_{\text{base}}(y)\) for the
83	  corresponding preimage \(y\).
84	
85	  \emph{Backward direction} threads the Galois connection between
86	  vanishing-ideal and support of an ideal sheaf. The proof has six
87	  steps; the Mathlib primitives cited are present at the project's
88	  pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}).
89	
90	  \begin{enumerate}
91	  \item \emph{Range of a closed immersion is its kernel support.}
92	    Because \(i\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) factors (up to
93	    isomorphism) as the canonical subscheme inclusion of its kernel
94	    ideal sheaf, so
95	    \(\mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}) = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
96	    (Mathlib:
97	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.range\_subschemeι} +
98	    \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toImage}.)
99	  \item \emph{Range of \(g\) lies in \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).}
100	    The unconditional inclusion
101	    \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
102	    is shipped (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.range\_subset\_ker\_support}); under
103	    \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\), \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
104	    equals \(\overline{\mathrm{range}\,g_{\text{base}}}\)
105	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.support\_ker}). Since
106	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\) is closed and contains
107	    \(\mathrm{range}(g)\) by hypothesis, the closure also lies in
108	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\):
109	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support} \subseteq i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
110	    (Mathlib: \texttt{closure\_minimal}.)
111	  \item \emph{\(Y\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(g.\mathrm{ker}\) is radical.}
112	    Under \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\),
113	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{ideal}\,U = \mathrm{ker}(g.\mathrm{app}\,U)\)
114	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.ker\_apply}). Reducedness of \(Y\) makes this
115	    ring kernel a radical ideal pointwise on each affine open, so
116	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical} = g.\mathrm{ker}\).
117	  \item \emph{\(Z\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(i.\mathrm{ker}\) is
118	    radical.} Identical argument with \(i\) in place of \(g\)
119	    (closed immersions are quasi-compact, so the
120	    \texttt{ker\_apply} hypothesis is satisfied automatically).
121	  \item \emph{Galois-connection chain.} Combining Steps~2--4 with
122	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.vanishingIdeal\_support} (which equates
123	    the vanishing ideal of a support with the radical) gives
124	    \[
125	      i.\mathrm{ker}
126	        = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
127	        = \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
128	        \le \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
129	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
130	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.
131	    \]
132	    The middle inequality uses the antitone half of the Galois
133	    connection (\texttt{IdealSheafData.gc}).
134	  \item \emph{Apply Mathlib's kernel-inequality lift.} The proof
135	    concludes by feeding \(\mathrm{hker} : i.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)
136	    to \texttt{IsClosedImmersion.lift} and reading off its
137	    factorisation lemma \texttt{lift\_fac}.
138	  \end{enumerate}
139	\end{proof}
140	
141	\section{Tensor reducedness over the algebraic closure}
142	\label{sec:tensor_AwayGm_isDomain}
143	
144	\begin{theorem}\leanok
145	[domain structure on \(\mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\)]
146	  \label{thm:gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
148	  Let \(\overline{k}\) be a field, let \(d \ge 1\), and let \(f\) be a
149	  non-zero homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d\) in the
150	  \(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading on
151	  \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\). Then the
152	  tensor product
153	  \[
154	    \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\bigl(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading}\,\overline{k}\bigr)\,f
155	    \;\otimes_{\overline{k}}\;
156	    \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
157	  \]
158	  is a domain (and hence reduced).
159	\end{theorem}
160	\begin{proof}
161	
162	\leanok
163	  The proof exhibits a canonical isomorphism of \(\overline{k}\)-algebras
164	  identifying the tensor product with the localisation of a
165	  polynomial ring over a domain at a non-zero-divisor; both operations
166	  preserve the domain property.
167	
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	  \item \emph{Domain instance on the homogeneous localisation.}
170	    \(\mathrm{Away}\,f := \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading})\,f\)
171	    embeds via the \texttt{val\_injective} bridge into
172	    \(\mathrm{Localization.Away}\,f\); since the polynomial ring
173	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\) is a domain
174	    and \(f \ne 0\), the localisation is a domain, and the bridge
175	    transports the property to \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\). (Already used in
176	    \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}; see
177	    \texttt{GmScaling.lean} L734--738 for the precise instance
178	    threading.)
179	  \item \emph{Collapse the tensor with the polynomial factor.}
180	    Recalling
181	    \(\mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
182	       = \mathrm{Localization.Away}(X()\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k})\),
183	    apply \texttt{MvPolynomial.algebraTensorAlgEquiv} to get
184	    \[
185	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k}
186	      \;\cong\;
187	      \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f),
188	    \]
189	    a \(\overline{k}\)-algebra (in fact \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\)-algebra)
190	    isomorphism.
191	  \item \emph{Push the localisation through the tensor.}
192	    Tensoring with the localisation step
193	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k} \to \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}\)
194	    factors through Mathlib's tensor-base-change lemma
195	    \texttt{IsLocalization.Away.tensorRightEquiv}, yielding an
196	    isomorphism
197	    \[
198	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
199	      \;\cong\;
200	      \mathrm{Localization.Away}(\iota(X())\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f))
201	    \]
202	    where \(\iota\) is the canonical algebra map. The threading uses
203	    \texttt{IsScalarTower} compatibility and the
204	    \texttt{Algebra}/\(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\) instance on the
205	    tensored polynomial ring.
206	  \item \emph{Right-hand side is a domain.}
207	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f)\) is a
208	    polynomial ring over a domain (Step~1), hence a domain by the
209	    standard instance. The generator \(X()\) is non-zero
210	    (\texttt{MvPolynomial.X\_ne\_zero}), so its powers form a
211	    submonoid of the non-zero-divisors
212	    (\texttt{powers\_le\_nonZeroDivisors\_of\_noZeroDivisors}); the
213	    localisation is then a domain by
214	    \texttt{IsLocalization.isDomain\_localization}.
215	  \end{enumerate}
216	
217	  Transferring the domain property along the iso of Step~3 closes the
218	  goal. Reducedness is a direct corollary of being a domain.
219	\end{proof}

25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}

31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a \(\Spec\) with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-\(1\) specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.

89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	
101	\leanok
102	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
103	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
104	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
105	  Let \(X\) be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
106	  let \(U \subseteq X\) be an open subset. For any \(z \in U\),
107	  \[
108	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
109	  \]
110	  where on the right \(U\) carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
111	  z \in U\rangle\( denotes \)z\( viewed as a point of \)U$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	
116	\leanok
117	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers \(n\), of the
118	  set of \(n\) for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
119	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the relevant
120	  specialisation preorder (with \(\prec\) denoting strict specialisation). It
121	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
122	  strict-generisation chains starting at \(z\) in \(X\) and those starting at the
123	  corresponding point of \(U\).
124	
125	  \emph{Chains in \(U\) embed into \(X\).} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow X\) is
126	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(U\)
127	  then \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(X\), and distinct points of \(U\) remain
128	  distinct in \(X\). Hence every strict-generisation chain in \(U\) starting at
129	  \(z\) lifts to one in \(X\) of the same length.
130	
131	  \emph{Chains in \(X\) starting at \(z\) all lie in \(U\).} Conversely, suppose
132	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) is a strict-generisation chain
133	  in \(X\) with \(a_0 = z \in U\). For each \(i \geq 0\), the relation $a_i \succeq
134	  z\( (in the specialisation preorder) is \)z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
135	  \(a_i\) is in the closure of \(\{z\}\) \emph{viewed from above}; in our
136	  convention the strict order \(a \prec b\) records \(b \rightsquigarrow a\)
137	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so \(a_i \succeq z\) in \(X\) means
138	  \(a_i \rightsquigarrow z\) in \(X\). By the Mathlib lemma
139	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
140	  \(a \rightsquigarrow z\) with \(z\) in an open set \(U\) forces \(a \in U\), since
141	  \(U\) is open and contains \(z\), hence contains every point whose closure
142	  contains \(z\) --- equivalently every \(a\) with \(a \rightsquigarrow z\)),
143	  every \(a_i\) lies in \(U\). Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
144	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
145	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle\( in \)U$ of the same length.
146	
147	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
148	  chains starting at \(z\) (resp.\ at \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\)) and
149	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
153	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
154	
155	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
156	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
157	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
158	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
159	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	
163	\leanok
164	  [Coheight on \(\Spec R\) equals height in \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)]
165	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
166	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
167	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(p \in \Spec R\) (viewed as a point of
168	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
169	  \[
170	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
171	  \]
172	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
173	  prime-ideal chains \(\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}\)
174	  ending at the prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) corresponding to \(p\).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	\leanok
180	  The specialisation preorder on \(\Spec R\) is the dual of the natural
181	  inclusion preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). Concretely, for
182	  \(p, q \in \Spec R\) with underlying primes \(\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}\),
183	  the relation \(p \leq q\) in the specialisation preorder (which is
184	  \(q \rightsquigarrow p\) in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
185	  containment \(\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}\). This is the content of
186	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
187	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
188	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on \(\Spec R\)
189	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)).
190	
191	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of \(p\) in \(\Spec R\)
192	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at \(p\),
193	  i.e.\ ascending chains \(p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the
194	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of \(\mathfrak{p}\) in
195	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\) (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
196	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
197	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
198	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
199	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
200	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
201	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
202	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
203	  identification \(\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}\)
204	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
205	\end{proof}
206	
207	\section{The coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
208	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
209	
210	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
211	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
212	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
213	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
214	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
215	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
216	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
217	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
218	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
219	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
220	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}
223	
224	\leanok
225	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
226	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
228	  
229	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be any point. Then
230	  \[
231	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
232	  \]
233	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at \(z\) equals the coheight of \(z\) in
234	  the underlying topological space of \(X\) (with respect to the specialisation
235	  preorder).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  
240	  \leanok
241	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
242	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
243	
244	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of \(z\).} By
245	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
246	  applied to the open set \(X\) itself, there exists an affine open
247	  \(U \subseteq X\) with \(z \in U\). Write \(\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U\)
248	  for \(z\) viewed as a point of the subscheme \(U\).
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to \(z\) inside \(U\).} Since
251	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\) via the affine equivalence
252	  \(U \simeq \Spec R\) (with \(R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\)), the point
253	  \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\) corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
254	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
255	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,\(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\).
256	
257	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of \(\Gamma(U)\) at
258	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The classical affine-stalk identification says
259	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}\). In Mathlib this is the
260	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
261	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\) as a localisation of \(R\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\) in the
262	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
263	
264	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
265	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The Mathlib lemma
266	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
267	  \[
268	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
269	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
270	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
271	  \]
272	  where \(\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})\) is the prime-ideal height in
273	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). By
274	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
275	  \(\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}\) used in
276	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
277	
278	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from \(U\) to \(X\).} By
279	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to \(R\) and the
280	  affine identification \(U \simeq \Spec R\),
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
283	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
284	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
285	  \]
286	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
287	  \(U \subseteq X\) promotes the right-hand side back to
288	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)\). Concatenating the equalities from
289	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
290	\end{proof}

293	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
294	
295	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
296	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
297	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
298	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-\(1\) case used in
299	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
300	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
301	
302	\begin{lemma}
303	
304	\leanok
305	  [Coheight \(= 1\) forces stalk \(\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1\)]
306	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
308	  
309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be a point with
310	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\). Then the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
311	  has Krull dimension at most \(1\), equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
312	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
313	\end{lemma}
314	
315	\begin{proof}
316	  
317	  \leanok
318	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
319	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\).
320	  The Mathlib predicate \(\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R\) unfolds to
321	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1\), so the inequality \(1 \leq 1\)
322	  discharges the goal.
323	\end{proof}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A

31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The
126	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} dependency is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
161	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  
150	\leanok
151	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
152	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
153	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
154	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
155	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
156	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
157	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
158	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
159	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
160	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
161	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
162	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
163	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
164	\end{proof}
165	
166	\section{Projective dimension}
167	\label{sec:ab_pd}
168	
169	\begin{definition}
170	
171	\leanok
172	  [Projective dimension]
173	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
174	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
175	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
176	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
177	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
178	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
179	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
180	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
181	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
182	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
183	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
184	  \[
185	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
186	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
187	  \]
188	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
189	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
190	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
191	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
192	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
193	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
194	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
195	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
196	\end{definition}
197	
198	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
199	\begin{itemize}
200	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
201	    iff \(M\) is free);
202	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
203	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
204	\end{itemize}
205	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
206	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
207	
208	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
209	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
210	
211	\begin{lemma}
212	
213	\leanok
214	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
215	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
216	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
217	  
218	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
219	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
220	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
221	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
222	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
223	  %  \begin{enumerate}
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
228	  %  \item
229	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
230	  %  \end{enumerate}"
231	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
232	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
233	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
234	  \(R\)-modules. Then
235	  \begin{enumerate}
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
237	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
238	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
239	  \end{enumerate}
240	\end{lemma}
241	
242	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
243	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
244	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
245	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
246	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
247	%  from Lemma REF."
248	\begin{proof}
249	  
250	  
251	  
252	  
253	  
254	\leanok
255	  By \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
256	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
257	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
258	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
259	  \[
260	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
261	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
262	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
263	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
264	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
265	  \]
266	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
267	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
268	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
269	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
273	\label{sec:ab_main}
274	
275	\begin{theorem}
276	
277	\leanok
278	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
279	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
280	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
281	  
282	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
283	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
284	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
285	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
286	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
287	  %  \(\)
288	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
289	  %  \(\)"
290	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
291	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
292	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
293	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
294	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
295	  \[
296	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
297	  \]
298	\end{theorem}
299	
300	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
301	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
302	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
303	%  \(\)
304	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
305	%  \(\)
306	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
307	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
308	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
309	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
310	%  Proposition REF we see that
311	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
312	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
313	%  \begin{align*}
314	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
315	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
316	%  \ldots,\\
317	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
318	%  \end{align*}
319	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
320	%  \begin{align*}
321	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
322	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\

345	%  M/xM \to 0

352	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
353	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
354	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
355	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective

358	  
359	  
360	  
361	\leanok
362	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
363	
364	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
365	  finite free resolution
366	  \[
367	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
368	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
369	  \]
370	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
371	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
372	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
373	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
374	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
375	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
376	  sequences
377	  \[
378	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
379	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
380	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
381	  \]
382	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
383	  yields the chain of inequalities
384	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
385	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
386	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
387	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
388	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
389	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
390	
391	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
392	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
393	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
394	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
395	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
396	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
397	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
398	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
399	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
400	  \[
401	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
402	  \]
403	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
404	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
405	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
406	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
407	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
408	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
409	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
410	  \[
411	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
412	  \]
413	  Substituting yields
414	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
415	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
416	\end{proof}

503	\subsection{Inductive-step substrate: per-gap decomposition of
504	  \texorpdfstring{\(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\)}{pd(M) = k+1}}
505	\label{subsec:succ_pd_gap_sequence}
506	
507	\paragraph{Status: RESOLVED iter-202.}
508	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} and the main formula
509	\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} are both closed axiom-clean as of
510	iter-202, by a \(\texttt{Nat}\)-induction restructuring
511	(\(\texttt{induction}\,k\,\texttt{generalizing}\,M\)): the inductive
512	step uses the kernel SES with
513	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) parts (2),(3) directly (no
514	exactness criterion), and the base case \(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\) uses
515	the iter-201 matrix-collapse helper
516	(\(\texttt{ext\_comp\_mk}_0\texttt{\_ofHom\_eq\_zero\_of\_entries\_mem\_annihilator}\))
517	together with the long exact sequence. \textbf{Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF,
518	the ``what is exact'' criterion) was OBVIATED} by this Path~B route ---
519	no Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion was needed. \textbf{All
520	gaps (1)--(4) are CLOSED or OBVIATED.} The decomposition below is
521	retained as the historical dependency analysis that motivated the
522	substrate builds; it is no longer an open work-list.
523	
524	\medskip
525	The inductive step
526	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
527	of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula was originally gated, against an
528	earlier pinned Mathlib snapshot, by three absent substrate ingredients
529	(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth in hand
530	(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
531	decomposition that the proof block of
532	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} folded into four bullets:
533	its purpose was to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort
534	estimates explicit, so that the substrate gaps could be formalised
535	independently (and where the dependency graph permitted, in parallel).
536	
537	\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are
538	\emph{not} mutually independent at the substrate level. Gap (3)
539	(snake-lemma carving of the minimal resolution by an
540	\(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\)) needs gap (1)
541	(minimal-resolution normalisation: every finite free resolution of
542	a finite module over a Noetherian local ring trims to a minimal one
543	of the same projective dimension) as input, because the snake-lemma
544	output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)
545	over \(R/(x)\) of the same length when the input resolution is itself
546	minimal. Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the ``what is exact'' criterion in
547	terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
548	independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
549	self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
550	feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).
551	
552	\paragraph{Dependency diagram.} Read top-to-bottom as ``provides
553	input to'':
554	\begin{verbatim}
555	                gap (1)
556	       minimal-resolution carving
557	   (Stacks lemma-add-trivial-complex)
558	                  |
559	                  v
560	                gap (3)
561	        snake lemma on minimal
562	        resolution under x in m
563	                  |
564	                  v
565	   +--------------+--------------+
566	   |   L1299 closure assembly    |   <--   gap (2)
567	   |   (succ-pd inductive step)  |        Stacks 00MF
568	   +-----------------------------+        "what is exact"
569	                  ^
570	                  |
571	                gap (4)
572	       depth drops by one (CLOSED;
573	       see lem:depth_drops_by_one)
574	\end{verbatim}
575	Gap (4) is already formalised
576	(\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}, the project's
577	\texttt{depth\_quotSMulTop\_succ\_eq\_depth\_of\_isSMulRegular})
578	and feeds the closure assembly directly. Gap (2) feeds the closure
579	assembly independently of the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3) chain.
580	
581	\paragraph{Per-gap effort estimates.} The following table records,
582	for each gap, the estimated formalisation effort (lines of code in
583	the project's Lean source style), a coarse iter budget, the
584	Mathlib-at-\texttt{b80f227} status, and substrate dependencies on
585	other gaps:
586	\begin{center}

588	    \hline
589	    Gap & Statement                              & LOC est.  & Iters
590	        & Mathlib status     & Depends on \\

593	        & per-syzygy step CLOSED iter-199
594	                                                                & none \\
